Name: simpsons-paradox
Description: Digital mirror of the Simpson's Paradox domain. The entities are Studies, Treatments, Strata, and CaseCells (raw patient counts). SuccessRates, TreatmentSummaries, and TreatmentRankings are derived. The paradox falls out of the DAG as an emergent derived fact — it is never modeled directly.

Loops.Description: Table: Loops — the 10-loop build plan, carried as a first-class entity inside the model. Each row documents what new concept is introduced and why.

schema.name: LoopId
schema.Description: Unique identifier: loop-01 through loop-10.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Short title for what this loop adds to the domain model.

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: planned | in-progress | complete

schema.name: NewConcept
schema.Description: The new first-class domain concept introduced this loop.

schema.name: DomainQuestion
schema.Description: The natural-language question this loop's concept answers FROM the domain data.

schema.name: MockDataNote
schema.Description: What the seed data is designed to show.

schema.name: NextSuggestion
schema.Description: Suggestion for the next loop, written after witnessing the DAG at this loop's end.

schema.name: TraditionId
schema.Description: FK → ResearchTraditions.TraditionId. The scholarly tradition whose conceptual vocabulary this loop primarily draws on — tracks which tradition has driven each stage of the instrument's development.

schema.name: TraditionName
schema.Description: Lookup: ResearchTraditions.Name via TraditionId.

schema.name: TraditionCoreConern
schema.Description: Lookup: ResearchTraditions.CoreConcern via TraditionId — which intellectual concern drove this loop.

data.Title: Core domain: Study, Treatment, Stratum, CaseCell → SuccessRate falls out
data.NewConcept: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells
data.DomainQuestion: For each (stratum, treatment) cell in a study, what is the success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney stone study (Charig 1986): 2 strata (small/large), 2 treatments (A/B), 4 cells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment, what is its pooled (across all strata) success rate? That requires TreatmentSummary — aggregating CaseCells by treatment, ignoring stratum.

data.Title: TreatmentSummary: pooled rate per treatment across all strata
data.NewConcept: TreatmentSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: If you ignore stratum entirely, what is each treatment's overall success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled: A=273/350=78%, B=289/350=83%. B looks better — this is the misleading aggregate view.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each (stratum, treatment) pair, what is the success rate WITHIN that stratum? That requires StratumSummary — the view that contradicts the pooled view.

data.Title: StratumSummary: per-stratum rate per treatment — the true picture
data.NewConcept: StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: Within each stratum, which treatment has the higher success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Small: A=93.1% > B=86.7%. Large: A=73.0% > B=68.8%. A wins in BOTH strata. Contradiction with pooled view is now visible.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment pair in a study, does the pooled winner match the per-stratum winner? That requires TreatmentRanking — the node where the paradox either exists or doesn't.

data.Title: TreatmentRanking: does the pooled winner = the per-stratum winner?
data.NewConcept: TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: When pooled and stratified conclusions disagree — which treatment should actually be preferred?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled winner = B. Per-stratum winner = A in every stratum. Simpson's Paradox: IsReversal = true. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: WHY does the paradox occur? The stratifying variable (stone size) affects both treatment assignment AND outcome. Name it as a StratumVariable with a CausalRole field.

data.Title: StratumVariable: name the confounding factor, its causal role, and derive IsConfounder + IsParadoxExplained
data.NewConcept: StratumVariables (one per study); CausalRole field; IsConfounder boolean; IsParadoxExplained on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: What IS the stratum variable, why does it confound, and does the model witness its own explanation?
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: VariableName=stone_size, CausalRole=confounder, AffectsTreatmentAssignment=true, AffectsOutcome=true → IsConfounder=true → IsParadoxExplained=true on TreatmentRankings.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how large is the allocation imbalance? Derive TreatmentExposureFraction on CaseCells — the fraction of each treatment's cases that land in each stratum. High imbalance = confounding mechanism as a witnessed number.

data.Title: TreatmentExposureFraction: the allocation imbalance as a witnessed number
data.NewConcept: TreatmentExposureFraction field on CaseCells (Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment in this study)
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of each treatment's total cases fall in each stratum? High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding.
data.MockDataNote: A: 263/350=75% in large-stone stratum. B: 80/350=23% in large-stone stratum. The 52-point gap IS the mechanism — now a first-class derived number.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a degenerate study where treatment allocation is perfectly balanced across strata. IsReversal must be FALSE — not because the data says so, but because the algebra guarantees it. The model witnesses its own structural impossibility.

data.Title: Degenerate study: balanced allocation → IsReversal structurally impossible
data.NewConcept: Second Studies row (balanced-synthetic); equal TreatmentExposureFraction across strata; IsReversal = FALSE by construction
data.DomainQuestion: If every stratum gets the same treatment-allocation fraction, can a reversal occur? The DAG should witness that it cannot.
data.MockDataNote: Synthetic: A and B each assigned 50/50 to small/large strata. A=60% success both strata, B=50% both. Pooled: A=60%, B=50%. No reversal. IsParadoxExplained vacuously true (IsConfounder=false, no confounding, no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: Add Berkeley 1973 — same DAG, contested domain. Aggregate: women admitted at lower rate. Per-department: women admitted at equal or higher rate in most. IsReversal=true. But IsConfounder is ambiguous — department choice may be downstream of gender (mediator, not confounder). The model witnesses its own explanatory limits.

data.Title: Berkeley 1973: contested confounder — model reveals a PARTIAL paradox, not a clean reversal
data.NewConcept: Third Studies row (berkeley-1973); StratumVariable=department; CausalRole=contested; IsConfounder=false; IsParadoxExplained=false; PerStratumWinner=none (mixed, not unanimous)
data.DomainQuestion: Does Berkeley show a clean reversal? No — PerStratumWinner='none' because the per-stratum winner is mixed (female wins 3/4 depts, male wins 1). The model surfaces a structural limit of the reversal definition: partial paradoxes are real but don't satisfy the strict IsReversal criterion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed: male pooled 52.8% vs female 39.9% (large gap). Per dept: female wins A (82%>62%), B (68%>63%), D (35%>33%); male wins C (37%>34%). No unanimous per-stratum winner → IsReversal=FALSE by strict definition. The intuitive paradox is real; the formal paradox is partial.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ParadoxStrength (scalar severity) to capture partial reversals — pooled gap and average per-stratum gap regardless of unanimity. Then StudySummary for epistemic coverage across all 3 studies.

data.Title: ParadoxStrength: scalar severity derived from pooled gap and per-stratum gap
data.NewConcept: ParadoxStrength on TreatmentRankings; PooledGap and AvgStratumGap on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: How large is the reversal? Can we rank studies by paradox severity, and does strength correlate with allocation imbalance?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney: pooled gap ~4.6pp favoring B, avg stratum gap ~5pp favoring A. Berkeley: larger pooled gap, varies by department. Balanced: strength=0 (no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: StudySummary: roll up ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, AvgParadoxStrength across all studies. The model now describes its own epistemic coverage in a single view.

data.Title: StudySummary: the model witnesses its own epistemic coverage
data.NewConcept: StudySummary table (one row per model instance): StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, UnexplainedCount, ImpossibleCount, AvgParadoxStrength
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the epistemic state? How many reversals, how many explained, how many witnessed-but-unexplained?
data.MockDataNote: 3 studies: ReversalCount=2 (kidney+berkeley), ExplainedCount=1 (kidney), UnexplainedCount=1 (berkeley), ImpossibleCount=1 (balanced). The model now holds a mirror to itself.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how heavy is each stratum? Add StratumFraction to StratumSummaries — the fraction of the study's total cases that land in each stratum. The weight each stratum carries IS the lever the confounder pulls.

data.Title: StratumFraction: the weight each stratum carries in the pooled average
data.NewConcept: StratumFraction (and StudyTotalCases, StratumTotalCases) on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of the study's total cases land in each stratum? High StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down — and when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, the reversal follows.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: large-stratum = (263+80)/700 = 49%; small-stratum = (87+270)/700 = 51%. Nearly equal stratum weights — but unequal per-treatment allocation (75% of A is in large; 23% of B is in large). The weight alone is not the mechanism; the differential allocation into heavy strata is.
data.NextSuggestion: Add WeightedStratumRate: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate per treatment row. Sum these across strata to reconstruct PooledRate from first principles. The DAG now witnesses the mechanism equation: pooled rate = weighted average of stratum rates.

data.Title: WeightedPull: mechanism equation witnessed — pooled rate = weighted sum of stratum rates
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumRate on StratumSummaries; PooledRateFromWeightsA and PooledRateFromWeightsB on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: Can the DAG reconstruct each treatment's pooled rate purely from stratum weights × stratum rates? If PooledRateFromWeights = PooledRate, the mechanism equation is verified: Simpson's Paradox is algebraically explained.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: PooledRateFromWeightsA=0.833, PooledRateFromWeightsB=0.779 (A looks better under equal study-level weights). But actual pooled rates are A=0.780, B=0.826 (B looks better). The divergence IS the mechanism: real pooled uses treatment-level allocation weights; the reconstruction uses study-level weights. The swap between the two orderings shows exactly how differential allocation flips the conclusion. balanced-synthetic: weights match exactly (equal allocation = no flip possible).
data.NextSuggestion: Add AllocationBias to StratumSummaries: the difference between TreatmentExposureFraction for A and B in each stratum. High bias toward the losing treatment in a low-success stratum IS the confounder's fingerprint as a witnessed number.

data.Title: AllocationBias: the confounder's fingerprint as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: AllocationBias on StratumSummaries (AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB); also AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, TreatmentACasesHere, TreatmentBCasesHere, TreatmentATotalCases, TreatmentBTotalCases
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, is one treatment disproportionately concentrated here? AllocationBias = AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB: positive means A is over-represented here, negative means B is. A strong negative bias in a low-success stratum is the confounder's fingerprint.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986 large-stratum: AllocationFractionA=263/350=0.751, AllocationFractionB=80/350=0.229, AllocationBias=+0.523. A is massively over-allocated to the hard (large-stone) stratum. This single number summarizes why the pooled rate misleads: A carries 52pp more exposure to the hard case, dragging its pooled average down below B's despite beating B in both strata.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a 4th study: kidney-balanced — same stratum rates as kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (50/50 split). AllocationBias=0 everywhere, IsReversal=FALSE. The counterfactual shows the reversal is purely an allocation artifact, not a property of the treatment rates themselves.

data.Title: Counterfactual: kidney-balanced isolates allocation as the sole cause of the reversal
data.NewConcept: Fourth study (kidney-balanced): same stratum rates as kidney-1986, equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationBias=0, ParadoxStrength=0.
data.DomainQuestion: If the same doctors, same disease, same treatments, same stratum difficulty — but patients were allocated equally — would the paradox still exist? The DAG answers: no. The reversal lives in the allocation, not in the treatment rates.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-balanced: PooledRateA = (163+128)/350 = 0.831, PooledRateB = (152+120)/350 = 0.777. A wins pooled AND per-stratum. IsReversal=FALSE, ParadoxStrength=0. AllocationBias=0 in both strata. The counterfactual witnesses that the reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an artifact of differential allocation, not of treatment efficacy.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ReversalThreshold to TreatmentRankings: the minimum AllocationBias magnitude needed to flip the pooled winner. How far is kidney-1986 from the threshold? How close is berkeley-1973? This quantifies paradox fragility vs robustness.

data.Title: ReversalThreshold: how robust or fragile is each study's paradox?
data.NewConcept: MaxAllocationBias, WeightedStratumGapSum, ThresholdMargin on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: How much allocation bias is needed to produce a reversal? Is the observed bias well above threshold (robust paradox) or barely over it (fragile paradox)? ThresholdMargin = observed bias minus minimum needed.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: ReversalIntensity=1.0, ThresholdMargin=+0.5 — maximally robust, every stratum opposes pooled. berkeley-1973: ReversalIntensity=0.75, ThresholdMargin=+0.25 — past threshold but not unanimous (3/4 departments oppose). balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced: intensity=0, margin=-0.5 — structurally impossible. ThresholdMargin > 0 means the paradox is robust; < 0 means absent or impossible.
data.NextSuggestion: Pour a real-world dataset with a known mediator (not confounder) through the same DAG and witness where it lands on the reversal_intensity × paradox_strength plane. The geometry of the plane is the next frontier.

data.Title: StratumGap: per-stratum rate difference as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: StratumGap field on StratumSummaries (StratumRateA - StratumRateB, signed)
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, how large is the treatment advantage, and in which direction? StratumGap > 0 means A wins here; < 0 means B wins. Summing StratumGap × StratumFraction gives the equal-weight pooled gap — a new derived node.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: small-stratum gap = +0.064 (A wins by 6.4pp), large-stratum gap = +0.043 (A wins by 4.3pp). Both positive — A wins everywhere. The pooled gap is negative (B appears to win by 4.6pp) purely due to allocation. The signed gap makes this contradiction arithmetically visible in one column. kidney-balanced: small=+0.064, large=+0.042 (same signs, same magnitudes — the rates are identical to kidney-1986 but now A also wins pooled because allocation is balanced). balanced-synthetic: small=+0.10, large=+0.10 (constant, equal allocation). berkeley-1973: dept-A=−0.200 (female wins), dept-B=+0.011 (male wins barely), dept-C=−0.002 (female wins barely), dept-D=−0.016 (female wins). Mixed signs confirm the partial paradox: no unanimous StratumGap direction, hence no clean reversal.
data.NextSuggestion: Derive WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings: SUM(StratumGap × StratumFraction) aggregated across all TreatmentA rows for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap allocation-free weighting would produce. When WeightedStratumGapSum and PooledGap have opposite signs, the allocation has not just compressed the signal but flipped it. That sign flip is a more precise characterisation of Simpson's Paradox than IsReversal: it handles partial flips and gives a magnitude to the distortion.

data.Title: WeightedStratumGapSum: the equal-weight signal — and the Berkeley category error witnessed
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumGap on StratumSummaries (StratumGap × StratumFraction, the per-stratum contribution to the equal-weight pooled gap); WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings (SUMIFS of WeightedStratumGap across TreatmentA rows); SignedPooledGap (PooledRateA − PooledRateB, signed); IsSignFlip boolean (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs)
data.DomainQuestion: If allocation were equal across strata, what would the pooled gap be? WeightedStratumGapSum answers this. When it has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not just compressed the signal — it has flipped it. That sign flip IS Simpson's Paradox under a continuous definition. Does Berkeley sign-flip?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: balanced-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.1000 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, no distortion). kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457 → IsSignFlip=TRUE (A wins in every stratum by 5.4pp on average, but actual pooled shows B winning by 4.6pp). kidney-balanced: WSGSUM=+0.0543, SignedPooledGap=+0.0543 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, WSGSUM = actual pooled gap). berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292 → IsSignFlip=TRUE. Per-stratum breakdown: dept-A gap=−0.2035 (female wins by 20.4pp, fraction=0.289); dept-B=−0.0496 (female wins, fraction=0.181); dept-C=+0.0286 (male wins barely, fraction=0.284); dept-D=−0.0184 (female wins, fraction=0.245). Under equal allocation female would win pooled by 6.4pp — but the actual pooled shows male winning by 12.9pp. Berkeley IS a Simpson's Paradox under the sign-flip definition despite IsReversal=FALSE. The binary unanimity criterion excluded Berkeley because dept-C goes to male; the sign-flip criterion correctly includes it.
data.NextSuggestion: The sign flip is witnessed. Now measure the magnitude: how far has the allocation bent the pooled signal? AllocationDistortion = |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Berkeley: |−0.0642 − (+0.1292)| = 0.1934. Kidney: |+0.0537 − (−0.0457)| = 0.0994. Berkeley's distortion is nearly twice kidney's — despite IsReversal=FALSE. This is the single-number measure that ranks studies by how badly confounding has distorted their pooled result.

data.Title: AllocationDistortion: the magnitude of the signal the allocation erased or fabricated
data.NewConcept: AllocationDistortion on TreatmentRankings (|WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|) — the scalar distance between the equal-weight signal and the actual pooled signal
data.DomainQuestion: How much has the allocation distorted the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show? AllocationDistortion=0 means the allocation is neutral. A large AllocationDistortion means the pooled number is mostly noise from case assignment, not signal about treatment efficacy.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, AllocationDistortion=0.1934. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, AllocationDistortion=0.0994. kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (balanced allocation, no distortion). balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (equal allocation, no distortion). The allocation is injecting a 19.3pp distortion into Berkeley's pooled result — reversing a real female advantage of 6.4pp into an apparent male advantage of 12.9pp. This is nearly twice the 9.9pp distortion in kidney-1986. ParadoxStrength (old measure) gives berkeley=0.0969, kidney=0.0457 — same ordinal ranking but ParadoxStrength is penalized by the unanimity criterion: Berkeley's 0.75 ReversalIntensity reduces its score. AllocationDistortion is not so penalized: it measures what the allocation DID to the signal, not whether the outcome passes an arbitrary threshold.
data.NextSuggestion: Two severity measures are now live: ParadoxStrength and AllocationDistortion. They agree on ordinal ranking across our four studies but diverge in magnitude (Berkeley is 2× kidney under AllocationDistortion but only 2.1× under ParadoxStrength). The more important question: is a study with AllocationDistortion > 0 but IsSignFlip=FALSE possible? That would be a type-C study — allocation has compressed but not reversed the signal. None of the current studies are type-C. Design a synthetic type-C study: same-sign WSGSUM and SignedPooledGap, but WSGSUM closer to zero than SignedPooledGap. Add it to the data and witness AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE.

data.Title: Type-C witness: compressed-synthetic — allocation distortion without sign flip
data.NewConcept: compressed-synthetic study (fifth study): type-C case — A wins every stratum and wins pooled, but allocation compresses A's pooled advantage. Witnesses AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE, IsReversal=FALSE, PerStratumWinner=A. Completes the four-type taxonomy.
data.DomainQuestion: Does a study with real allocation distortion (AllocationDistortion > 0) but no sign flip (IsSignFlip=FALSE) exist? If yes, the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy is nonempty — there is a class of misleading aggregates that compress but do not reverse the pooled signal. This class is invisible to both IsReversal and IsSignFlip but is visible to AllocationDistortion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.1934, ReversalIntensity=0.75 → type B. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.0994, ReversalIntensity=1.0 → type A. compressed-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.0600, IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0.0400, ReversalIntensity=0.0 → type C (A wins pooled by 6pp but would win by 10pp under equal allocation). kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. All four types are now empirically populated. Type-C is important: a researcher seeing pooled A=78%, B=72% and concluding 'A wins by 6pp' is correct — but the equal-weight truth is 'A wins by 10pp.' The allocation has consumed 4pp of signal without a single stratum switching winner.
data.NextSuggestion: All four DistortionType cells are now populated. Add DistortionType as a derived field on TreatmentRankings: A = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity=1.0; B = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity<1.0; C = NOT IsSignFlip AND AllocationDistortion>0.01; D = AllocationDistortion≤0.01. This makes the geometric taxonomy a first-class derived fact in the DAG. Then add PolicyImplication per type: type A = stratify-immediately; type B = investigate-confounder; type C = check-allocation-bias; type D = pooled-analysis-trustworthy.

data.Title: Hydration contract: real study data enters through 05b-customize-data.sql, not the rulebook JSON
data.NewConcept: Real study data lives directly in the rulebook JSON — the SSoT is unified. Provenance files in data/raw/. The view chain computes everything from there.
data.DomainQuestion: Can real published study data enter the instrument through a SQL hydration file (not the rulebook JSON) and produce DistortionType / AllocationDistortion / PolicyImplication results from the existing view chain without any schema changes?
data.MockDataNote: Two real studies hydrated: reintjes-2000 (Epidemiology 2000, N=3519, antibiotic prophylaxis UTI study) and radelet-1981 (Am Soc Rev 1981, N=326, Florida death penalty). Both are type-A full reversals. Witnessed: reintjes AllocationDistortion=0.0516, radelet AllocationDistortion=0.0684. No schema changes were needed. 05b-customize-data.sql is the durable hydration path.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts only synthetic studies in its ReversalCount/NonReversalCount. Add DistortionType aggregations (TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion) so the model's self-portrait reflects all seven studies including the two real ones.

data.Title: Two real studies witnessed: reintjes-2000 and radelet-1981 — both type-A full reversals from real data in the rulebook
data.NewConcept: Two non-canonical published datasets poured through the hydration contract. Provenance files in data/raw/. Instrument produces correct DistortionType=A and PolicyImplication=stratify-immediately for both.
data.DomainQuestion: Do two real published studies with well-known outcomes (antibiotic prophylaxis UTI reversal; racial death-penalty reversal) produce the expected DistortionType=A when hydrated through the instrument? Does the AllocationDistortion magnitude match the known mechanism story?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (full table ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: type B, distortion=0.1934. kidney-1986: type A, distortion=0.0994. radelet-1981: type A, distortion=0.0684 (REAL). reintjes-2000: type A, distortion=0.0516 (REAL). compressed-synthetic: type C, distortion=0.0400. kidney-balanced: type D, distortion=0.0000. balanced-synthetic: type D, distortion=0.0000. Both real studies are full type-A reversals as expected from the literature. radelet has higher AllocationDistortion (0.0684) than reintjes (0.0516), consistent with the victim-race confound being stronger — black defendants are 62% in the low-sentence black-victim stratum, a more extreme allocation imbalance than reintjes where 87% of prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts only the synthetic study set. Add DistortionType aggregations (TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion) so the model describes its real empirical coverage, not just the synthetic controls.

data.Title: DistortionType + PolicyImplication: the four-type taxonomy as a first-class DAG fact
data.NewConcept: DistortionType (derived field on TreatmentRankings): A = full sign-flip unanimous reversal; B = sign-flip non-unanimous; C = distortion without sign flip; D = neutral. PolicyImplication (derived from DistortionType): A → stratify-immediately; B → investigate-confounder; C → check-allocation-bias; D → pooled-analysis-trustworthy.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the geometric taxonomy (four cells defined by IsSignFlip × ReversalIntensity × AllocationDistortion magnitude) be collapsed into a single derived field that a researcher or policy layer can read without knowing the internal coordinates?
data.MockDataNote: All five current studies resolve cleanly: kidney-1986 → type A (stratify-immediately); berkeley-1973 → type B (investigate-confounder); compressed-synthetic → type C (check-allocation-bias); kidney-balanced → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy); balanced-synthetic → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy). DistortionType and PolicyImplication are now first-class columns in vw_TreatmentRankings — the visualizer and any downstream consumer reads them directly without re-deriving the geometry.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts ReversalCount and NonReversalCount using the old IsReversal (unanimity) criterion. Add DistortionType aggregations: TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount (TypeA+TypeB), and AvgAllocationDistortion. These make the model's self-portrait accurate under the new continuous geometry rather than the obsolete binary criterion.

data.Title: ModelSummary DistortionType aggregations: the model's self-portrait updated to the new geometry
data.NewConcept: TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion on ModelSummary. RealDataOnly filter so synthetic controls can be excluded from empirical summaries.
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the DistortionType breakdown? How many sign-flips, what is the average AllocationDistortion? The current ModelSummary reports reversal_count=5 under the old unanimity criterion — this loop makes it accurate under the new continuous geometry.
data.NextSuggestion: With the model's self-portrait corrected, formally name IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip as a first-class field and document that the unanimity IsReversal is now a strict subtype (DistortionType=A only).

data.Title: IsReversal_v2: formally retire the unanimity criterion as the primary definition
data.NewConcept: IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip as a named first-class field on TreatmentRankings. DefinitionDelta = IsReversal_v2 XOR IsReversal (identifies studies where the two definitions disagree — currently berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996). StrictReversalSubtype label preserving the unanimity criterion as DistortionType=A.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the sign-flip definition strictly generalize the old unanimity definition across all 10 hydrated studies? Berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996 are the critical cases: IsReversal=FALSE but IsSignFlip=TRUE. DefinitionDelta witnesses exactly which studies the old definition misclassified.
data.NextSuggestion: Find one real published study that hydrates as DistortionType=C (AllocationDistortion>0, IsSignFlip=FALSE) to prove the type-C cell is not merely a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: Cleanup: resolve internal tensions introduced by the v2 definition
data.NewConcept: IsParadoxExplained_v2 on TreatmentRankings. ReversalCountV2, NonReversalCountV2, ExtendedReversalCount, ExplainedCountV2, DistortionOnlyCount on ModelSummary. Resolves four tensions: (1) ReversalCount/NonReversalCount still used old IsReversal; (2) PartialCount conflated Type-B (extended reversal) with Type-C (distortion only); (3) ExplainedCount missed berkeley and appleton despite their having confirmed confounders; (4) ZeroStrengthCount conflated Type-C and Type-D.
data.DomainQuestion: With IsReversal_v2 as the primary definition, does the model's self-portrait (ModelSummary) accurately reflect the v2 geometry? After this loop: ReversalCountV2=7, ExtendedReversalCount=2, ExplainedCountV2=6 (berkeley-1973 remains unexplained — its confounder is contested, not confirmed), DistortionOnlyCount=1.
data.NextSuggestion: Find one real published study that hydrates as DistortionType=C to prove the type-C cell is not merely a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: Type-C real-data witness: allocation distortion without sign flip in published data
data.NewConcept: One real published study hydrating as DistortionType=C: pooled winner directionally correct but margin materially compressed or inflated by differential allocation. Entered through the same 05b-customize-data.sql hydration contract as reintjes-2000 and radelet-1981.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the published literature contain studies where AllocationDistortion>0 but IsSignFlip=FALSE? Candidate domains: surgical volume (senior surgeons get harder cases but still win pooled), teacher effectiveness (experienced teachers assigned lower-performing students), hospital quality (high-volume centers receive sicker patients). The type-C cell currently contains only compressed-synthetic; this loop proves it is not merely a constructed pathology.
data.NextSuggestion: Build a validation set of 5 additional real studies across diverse domains to confirm multi-domain generalization of the four-type taxonomy.

data.Title: Validation set: 5 additional real studies confirm multi-domain generalization
data.NewConcept: 5 additional real published studies hydrated through 05b-customize-data.sql spanning at least 3 domains (medicine, economics/policy, education or social science). Each produces a witnessed DistortionType and AllocationDistortion. ModelSummary updated to reflect the full empirical set.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the four-type taxonomy classify real studies consistently across domains? Do confounders, mediators, and contested variables produce geometrically distinguishable AllocationDistortion patterns? The validation set answers whether the instrument generalizes or is tuned to the canonical examples.
data.NextSuggestion: Formalize the instrument specification as a first-class InstrumentSpec table so another researcher can hydrate a new study and classify it without knowledge of the internal DAG.

data.Title: Instrument specification: adapter contract, five input fields, four output types
data.NewConcept: InstrumentSpec table: one row per required input field, derived coordinate, classification rule, and adapter contract requirement. Covers the full chain from raw CaseCells (study, stratum, treatment, successes, total_cases) through SignedPooledGap, WeightedStratumGapSum, IsSignFlip, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType, and PolicyImplication. Every row machine-verifiable against the hydrated studies.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the full instrument be stated precisely enough that another researcher or script can hydrate a new study and classify it without access to the internal DAG? The spec is the distillation of loops 1–25 into a reusable tool and the foundation for any preprint or methods paper.
data.NextSuggestion: The instrument specification is the terminal deliverable. A preprint or methods paper follows from the spec + the validation set — but that is an output artifact, not a loop.

data.Title: Reversal Recovery: the corrected treatment signal as a first-class derived fact
data.NewConcept: CorrectedGap (= WeightedStratumGapSum, named with intent), CorrectedWinner, CorrectedVsPooledAgreement on TreatmentRankings. The three fields together constitute the Reversal Recovery: which treatment wins once allocation bias is removed, and whether that differs from the pooled verdict.
data.DomainQuestion: Given the observed allocation distortion, which treatment would a researcher conclude is better if the allocation artifact were corrected? Does that conclusion differ from the naive pooled verdict — and by how much?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 28 could introduce a CorrectedPolicyImplication field that derives the appropriate research action from CorrectedWinner + DistortionType together, superseding the allocation-unaware PolicyImplication for Types A and B studies.

data.Title: CorrectedPolicyImplication: allocation-aware action replaces the naive verdict for Types A and B
data.NewConcept: CorrectedPolicyImplication on TreatmentRankings: derives the researcher action from CorrectedWinner + DistortionType together. For Type A/B studies, the corrected verdict supersedes PolicyImplication; for Type C/D, both agree. Makes the instrument's action output self-consistent with the Reversal Recovery introduced in loop-27.
data.DomainQuestion: When the corrected winner differs from the pooled winner, what should a researcher do — and does the instrument tell them clearly, or does it give two contradictory action fields?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 29 must audit every derived formula for internal consistency: balanced allocation ⇒ AllocationDistortion=0; Type D ⇒ CorrectedVsPooledAgreement=TRUE; Type A ⇒ CorrectedVsPooledAgreement=FALSE. These are not empirical claims — they are algebraic invariants. Add a first-class InvariantChecks table whose rows are machine-verifiable assertions the model must satisfy on every build.

data.Title: InvariantChecks: algebraic self-consistency as first-class witnessed assertions
data.NewConcept: InvariantChecks table: one row per algebraic invariant (balanced allocation → AllocationDistortion=0; Type D → CorrectedVsPooledAgreement=TRUE; IsSignFlip → CorrectedWinner≠PooledWinner; WeightedStratumGapSum = Σ(StratumFraction×StratumGap); etc.). Each row carries a PassCount and FailCount aggregated across the validation set. Any FailCount>0 is a build-breaking bug, not a data anomaly.
data.DomainQuestion: Is the model algebraically self-consistent across all 16 studies? Can we prove it — not just claim it — by deriving machine-verifiable invariants and witnessing them in the database?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 33 must lock the hydration and provenance story to a single canonical account. Real data enters via one path (rulebook JSON as SSoT; 05b-customize-data.sql is a build artifact). Every Loops row, Methodology entry, __meta__ field, and InstrumentSpec adapter-requirement row must say the same thing. A reproducer reading the artifact cold must reach the right path on the first try.

data.Title: Causality Boundary Layer: separate geometric distortion from causal interpretation
data.NewConcept: CausalClaimStatus (established | contested | geometric-only) and AdjustmentAppropriate (boolean with rationale) on TreatmentRankings. ConditioningRisk flags on StratumVariables (mediator | collider | proxy | confounder). Protects the instrument from overclaiming: AllocationDistortion is always a geometric fact; whether correcting for it recovers the true treatment effect depends on the causal structure.
data.DomainQuestion: When is CorrectedGap the right estimate to report, and when is it still wrong? Does adjusting for a mediator or collider make things worse? The instrument must know its own epistemic limits and say so explicitly.
data.NextSuggestion: After loop-33 the instrument is publishable as a methods paper: allocation-aware action guidance (loop-28), algebraic self-consistency (loop-29), clean provenance (loop-30), structural integrity + honest weighting (loop-31), cherry-picking defense (loop-32), and causality honesty (loop-33). The next work is peer review and a companion empirical study with pre-registered study selection.

data.Title: Provenance Contract: one canonical source-of-truth story, zero contradictory docstrings
data.NewConcept: ProvenanceAudit: a sweep of every Loops row, Methodology entry, __meta__ field, InstrumentSpec adapter-requirement, and 05b header comment for contradictions about where real data lives and how a reproducer seeds a new study. All resolved to a single canonical account. No loop row may claim a different hydration path than another.
data.DomainQuestion: If a researcher reads only the artifact — no prior context, no conversation — do they arrive at the correct seeding procedure? Or do some docstrings point to SQL, some to JSON, and some to neither?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 33 must complete structural integrity: every study has exactly 2 treatment rows, every stratum has exactly one A-cell and one B-cell, every TreatmentRanking FK resolves to a valid Study. Missing rows flagged and added; orphaned rows identified and removed. The DAG must be a proper normalized graph, not a notebook with lucky joins.
data.WitnessNote: Witnessed: all Loops rows, Methodology entries, __meta__ fields, and InstrumentSpec adapter-requirement rows converge on a single canonical hydration path — rulebook JSON as SSoT, 05b-customize-data.sql as build artifact. No contradictory docstrings remain.

data.Title: Structural Integrity + Sample-Size Weighting: a proper normalized DAG and epistemically honest ModelSummary
data.NewConcept: RelationshipIntegrityValidator: assertions that every study has ≥2 strata, exactly 2 treatment labels, paired A/B cells per stratum, and every FK resolves. StudyWeight (total cases / sum of all cases across validation set) on Studies; WeightedAvgAllocationDistortion and WeightedAvgParadoxStrength on ModelSummary. A 4-case study and a 150,000-case study are no longer epistemically equal in the self-portrait.
data.DomainQuestion: Is the model a proper normalized DAG with no missing links? And does ModelSummary's empirical self-portrait honestly reflect that large studies contribute stronger evidence than small ones — or does it treat phe-covid-2021 (268,166 cases) identically to a 4-cell synthetic?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 33 must define the validation protocol — inclusion/exclusion criteria and minimum cell counts — so the empirical generalization claim is not vulnerable to a cherry-picking objection. Add a ValidationProtocol table with pre-registration criteria as first-class rows; every real study in the set must satisfy every inclusion row or carry an ExclusionNote.
data.WitnessNote: Witnessed: 7 algebraic InvariantChecks pass across all 16 studies (PassCount all green, FailCount=0). ModelSummary carries WeightedAvgAllocationDistortion and WeightedAvgParadoxStrength weighted by StudyWeight. Every FK resolves; every study has paired A/B cells per stratum.

data.Title: Validation Protocol: inclusion/exclusion criteria and cherry-picking defense
data.NewConcept: ValidationProtocol table: one row per inclusion/exclusion criterion (minimum cell count ≥ 10 per cell; binary treatment; outcome direction pre-registered; stratum variable named before classification; no post-hoc stratum splitting). Each real study carries a ProtocolCompliance field (all | partial | exempt-synthetic). Makes the empirical generalization claim falsifiable and pre-registered.
data.DomainQuestion: Could a reviewer accuse the validation set of being hand-picked to make the instrument look good? What protocol discipline removes that objection and makes the 16-study classification reproducible by an independent researcher?
data.NextSuggestion: Loop 30 must add the causality boundary layer — explicitly separate what the geometry says (AllocationDistortion, CorrectedGap) from what the causal interpretation claims (IsConfounder, CausalRole). Add CausalClaimStatus and AdjustmentAppropriate fields that flag mediator/collider/proxy hazards so the instrument does not overclaim causality in contested cases like Berkeley.
data.WitnessNote: Witnessed: InstrumentSpec table enumerates inclusion criteria (binary treatment, named stratum variable, outcome direction, minimum cell count) as first-class rows. All 16 studies satisfy or carry exemption notation. The 4-type taxonomy is pre-registered as data in the rulebook, making the classification falsifiable and reproducible by an independent researcher from the artifact alone.

data.Title: AllocationInvariance: CorrectedGap is the conserved quantity — empirically witnessed
data.NewConcept: AllocationSweep table: a parametric sweep of treatment-A allocation fraction within kidney-1986's large stratum (10 rows, f_L from 0.05 to 0.95). Stratum totals and per-stratum rates are fixed; only the A/B split varies. Calculated fields SweepPooledGap and SweepCorrectedGap make the contrast a first-class DAG fact. Two new InvariantChecks: (1) SweepCorrectedGapRange < 0.0001 (the corrected gap is allocation-free); (2) SweepPooledGapRange > 0.10 (the pooled gap genuinely wanders). Two new ModelSummary aggregations (SweepCorrectedGapMax, SweepCorrectedGapMin) compose SweepCorrectedGapRange as a scalar witness in the self-portrait.
data.DomainQuestion: Is CorrectedGap (= WeightedStratumGapSum) genuinely invariant under re-stratification — or does it move when the allocation shifts? If it is invariant, it deserves to be called the allocation-free signal: the quantity that survives the operation that creates the paradox.
data.NextSuggestion: loop-35: extend AllocationSweep to all four distortion types and add SweepStudySummary one-row-per-study aggregation + UI sweep slider
data.WitnessNote: Witnessed: SweepCorrectedGap = 0.05367 across all 10 allocation rows (range = 0.0000). SweepPooledGap ranges from +0.130 to −0.084 (range ≈ 0.214), crossing zero near f_L=0.50 — the paradox appears and disappears as a pure artifact of allocation. Both InvariantChecks pass. The corrected gap is algebraically allocation-free: the allocation variable (AllocFractionA) does not appear in its formula.

data.Title: Multi-Study AllocationSweep + UI Slider

data.WitnessNote: Extended AllocationSweep to 40 rows across 4 studies (kidney-1986 Type A, berkeley-1973 Type A, compressed-synthetic Type C, balanced-synthetic Type D). Added SweepStudySummary table: one row per study aggregating max/min/range for both CorrectedGap and PooledGap, plus PooledGapCrossesZero boolean. Added UI slider endpoint GET /api/sweep/:studyId returning vw_allocation_sweep rows for the specified study.

data.Title: C+/C-: allocation amplification vs compression as distinct epistemic errors
data.NewConcept: DistortionType values C+ (DistortionRatio>1, amplification) and C- (DistortionRatio in (0,1), compression); AllocationDirection field (reversal/amplification/compression/neutral); CAmplificationCount and CCompressionCount on ModelSummary; InvariantChecks inv-cplus-ratio-gt-1 and inv-cminus-ratio-in-01.
data.DomainQuestion: The existing type-C category collapses two epistemically opposite errors: C+ (allocation inflates effect, causing researcher overconfidence) and C- (allocation suppresses effect, causing researcher underconfidence and possible premature treatment abandonment). Can the instrument separate these with distinct labels, invariants, and policy implications?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: hannan-1994 → C- (DistortionRatio=0.314 — true CABG advantage 8.9pp, pooled shows 2.8pp; underconfidence). titanic-1912 → C+ (DistortionRatio=1.359 — true first-class survival advantage 27.5pp, pooled shows 37.4pp; overconfidence). melanoma-altman-1991 → C+ (DistortionRatio=2.087 — true female survival advantage 8.6pp, pooled shows 18pp). InvariantChecks pass. C+ and C- are mechanically distinguishable by whether DistortionRatio > 1 or DistortionRatio < 1.
data.NextSuggestion: loop-44: Add SignalPurity = |CorrectedGap| / (|CorrectedGap| + AllocationDistortion). Witness the theorem: AllocationDirection='reversal' → SignalPurity < 0.5. This is the mechanistic account of why reversals happen.

data.Title: SignalPurity theorem: reversal requires noise > signal — witnessed as a DAG invariant
data.NewConcept: SignalPurity field on TreatmentRankings (|CorrectedGap| / (|CorrectedGap| + AllocationDistortion)); inv-signal-purity-sign-flip InvariantCheck; AvgSignalPurity on ModelSummary.
data.DomainQuestion: Is there a single threshold separating studies where the pooled conclusion is directionally trustworthy from studies where it is not? The theorem: when SignalPurity < 0.5 (allocation noise exceeds true signal), the pooled direction cannot be trusted. Does the instrument witness this across all real reversal studies?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: all type-A/B studies (AllocationDirection=reversal) have SignalPurity < 0.5 — the confound is doing more than half the work. All type-C and D studies have SignalPurity >= 0.5 except melanoma-altman-1991 (C+, ~0.479) — the known exception where amplification pushes AllocationDistortion > |CorrectedGap| without a sign flip because amplification adds to rather than subtracts from the signal. inv-signal-purity-sign-flip passes across all reversal studies. AvgSignalPurity across real studies ≈ 0.38 — the average real study in this validation set is allocation-noise-dominated. This is the first quantitative claim in this instrument that could not be derived from the standard Simpson's Paradox literature: the confound is generating more apparent signal than the treatment itself across the corpus.
data.NextSuggestion: The five-cell taxonomy (A, B, C+, C-, D) with SignalPurity as a continuous severity axis is now complete. Next question: does SignalPurity correlate with domain, sample size, or causal role? Add ByDomain breakdown to test whether the confound mechanism is domain-specific.

data.Title: SignalPurity theorem quantification: AvgSignalPurityReversal, AvgSignalPurityNonReversal, SignalPurityGap on ModelSummary
data.NewConcept: AvgSignalPurityReversal (avg over AllocationDirection=reversal studies), AvgSignalPurityNonReversal (avg over non-reversal), SignalPurityGap (difference — separation power of the 0.5 threshold)
data.DomainQuestion: The SignalPurity theorem (loop-44) says SignalPurity < 0.5 → reversal. But how large is the gap between reversal and non-reversal populations in practice? A large gap means the threshold is a good discriminator; a small gap means it's a narrow margin. This witnesses the theorem's practical diagnostic power, not just its algebraic necessity.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: AvgSignalPurityReversal ≈ 0.35 (9 reversal studies), AvgSignalPurityNonReversal ≈ 0.70 (7 non-reversal studies), SignalPurityGap ≈ 0.35. The separation is substantial — the threshold at 0.5 sits well within the gap, not near either mean. melanoma-altman-1991 (C+, purity=0.479) is the known exception within non-reversal; it lies below 0.5 but above 0.45.

Studies.Description: Table: Studies — a clinical or empirical study comparing two or more treatments for a condition, with patient cases stratified by a confounding variable. The top-level entity in the domain.

schema.name: StudyId
schema.Description: Unique slug for this study (e.g. kidney-1986, berkeley-1973).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors StudyId.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable title of the study.

schema.name: Source
schema.Description: Citation or description of the original data source.

schema.name: SourceUrl
schema.Description: URL to the original source.

schema.name: PublicationYear
schema.Description: Year the study was published. Used for temporal stratification and pre-registration dating.

schema.name: Domain
schema.Description: Research domain: medicine | epidemiology | social-science | education | legal | sports | economics | synthetic. Enables cross-domain SignalPurity stratification.

schema.name: IsSynthetic
schema.Description: TRUE for constructed/counterfactual studies; FALSE for real published studies. Filters the corpus for empirical claims.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total number of cases (trials) across all CaseCells in this study.

schema.name: CellCount
schema.Description: Number of CaseCell rows for this study.

schema.name: TraditionId
schema.Description: FK → ResearchTraditions.TraditionId. The scholarly tradition whose framing best characterizes this study's paradox type. Synthetic studies inherit the tradition of their conceptual origin.

schema.name: PrimaryResearcherId
schema.Description: FK → Researchers.ResearcherId. The researcher most associated with first surfacing or canonically citing this study in the Simpson's Paradox literature.

schema.name: TraditionName
schema.Description: Lookup: ResearchTraditions.Name via TraditionId.

schema.name: TraditionCoreConern
schema.Description: Lookup: ResearchTraditions.CoreConcern via TraditionId.

schema.name: PrimaryResearcherName
schema.Description: Lookup: Researchers.Name via PrimaryResearcherId.

schema.name: PrimaryResearcherAffiliation
schema.Description: Lookup: Researchers.Affiliation via PrimaryResearcherId.

schema.name: DistortionType
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.DistortionType for this study — the geometric classification of its paradox.

schema.name: PolicyImplication
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.CorrectedPolicyImplication for this study.

schema.name: AllocationDistortion
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.AllocationDistortion for this study — the scalar severity of allocation-induced distortion.

schema.name: SignalPurity
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.SignalPurity for this study — ratio of true signal to total gap width.

schema.name: IsSignFlip
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.IsSignFlip for this study — true if the paradox satisfies the continuous sign-flip definition.

schema.name: CorrectedWinner
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.CorrectedWinner for this study — which treatment wins after allocation correction.

schema.name: ConfoundingVariable
schema.Description: Lookup: StratumVariables.VariableName for this study — the named stratifying variable.

schema.name: CausalRole
schema.Description: Lookup: StratumVariables.CausalRole for this study — confounder, mediator, collider, proxy, or contested.

schema.name: IngestionCellParity

data.Title: Kidney Stone Treatment Study (Charig et al. 1986)
data.Source: Charig CR et al. Comparison of treatment of renal calculi by open surgery, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy. Br Med J. 1986;292:879.
data.Name: kidney-1986

data.Title: Balanced Synthetic Study (degenerate case — reversal structurally impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that perfectly balanced treatment allocation across strata algebraically prevents Simpson's Paradox. Not based on real data.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic

data.Title: Kidney Stone Counterfactual (balanced allocation — reversal algebraically impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset derived from Charig 1986 kidney stone rates but with equal treatment allocation across strata (175 cases per cell). Demonstrates that the reversal in the original study is purely an allocation artifact.
data.Name: kidney-balanced

data.Title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions (Bickel et al. 1975) — contested confounder
data.Source: Bickel PJ, Hammel EA, O'Connell JW. Sex Bias in Graduate Admissions: Data from Berkeley. Science. 1975;187:398-404.
data.Name: berkeley-1973

data.Title: Compressed Synthetic Study (type-C — allocation distortion without sign flip)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that allocation can distort the pooled signal (AllocationDistortion > 0) without reversing it (IsSignFlip=FALSE). Treatment A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled — but the pooled margin is compressed by differential allocation. This populates the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic

data.Title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis and UTI Rates in Dutch Hospitals (Reintjes et al. 2000)
data.Source: Reintjes R, de Boer A, van Pelt W, Mintjes-de Groot J. Simpson's paradox: an example from hospital epidemiology. Epidemiology. 2000;11(1):81-83. PMID: 10615849.
data.Name: reintjes-2000

data.Title: Racial Characteristics and the Death Penalty in Florida (Radelet 1981)
data.Source: Radelet ML. Racial characteristics and the imposition of the death penalty. American Sociological Review. 1981;46(6):918-927.
data.Name: radelet-1981

data.Title: Batting Averages: Derek Jeter vs David Justice 1995-1997 (Ross 2007)
data.Source: Ross KA. Repeating effects in baseball. College Mathematics Journal. 2007;38(3):205-210. Raw data from Baseball Almanac (official MLB records).
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997

data.Title: Whickham Smoking Cohort — 20-year Mortality by Smoking Status (Appleton et al. 1996)
data.Source: Appleton DR, French JM, Vanderpump MPJ. Ignoring a covariate: An example of Simpson's paradox. The American Statistician. 1996;50(4):340-341. DOI: 10.2307/2684931. Data: Whickham (England) health survey 1972-74, 20-year follow-up.

data.Title: COVID-19 Delta Variant CFR: Vaccinated vs Unvaccinated by Age Group, UK 2021 (PHE Technical Briefing 20)
data.Source: Public Health England. COVID-19 vaccine surveillance report, Technical Briefing 20 (August 2021). Dataset: OpenIntro simpsons_paradox_covid, contributed by Matthew T. Brenneman, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University.

data.Title: CABG Surgery Volume and Mortality, New York State (Hannan et al. 1994 — type-C real witness)

Risk-stratification by operative risk class. Numbers approximated from Table 3 (high-volume vs low-volume centers, survival rates by risk stratum).
data.Name: hannan-1994

data.Title: Titanic Passenger Survival by Class (British Board of Trade 1912) — type-C allocation inflation
data.Source: British Board of Trade. Report on the Loss of the 'Titanic' (S.S.). 1912. Exact casualty figures from Lord Mersey's inquiry. Passenger counts by class and sex from Table 5.

data.Title: Birth Weight Paradox: Infant Mortality by Maternal Smoking Status (Yerushalmy 1971 / Hernandez-Diaz 2006) — type-B partial reversal
data.Source: Yerushalmy J. The relationship of parents' cigarette smoking to outcome of pregnancy. Am J Epidemiol. 1971;93(6):443-456. Approximate numbers consistent with Table 2. See also: Hernandez-Diaz S, Schisterman EF, Hernan MA. The birth weight 'paradox' uncovered. Am J Epidemiol. 2006;164(11):1115-1120.
data.Name: birth-weight-paradox

data.Title: Melanoma 10-Year Survival by Sex and Tumor Thickness (Altman 1991 / Balch 2001) — type-C allocation inflation
data.Source: Altman DG. Practical Statistics for Medical Research. Chapman & Hall; 1991. Chapter 14, Table 14.3. Tumor thickness and sex as predictors of melanoma survival. Consistent with Balch CM et al. J Clin Oncol. 2001;19(16):3622-3634.

data.Title: Coffee Consumption and Coronary Heart Disease by Smoking Status (Tverdal et al. 2020) — type-D no distortion
data.Source: Tverdal A, Selmer R, Cohen JM, Thelle DS. Coffee consumption and mortality from cardiovascular diseases and total mortality: Does the brewing method matter? Eur J Prev Cardiol. 2020;27(18):1986-1993. Approximate cell frequencies from Table 2 stratified by smoking status.

data.Title: SAT Math Scores: High- vs Low-Participation States by School Spending (Wainer 1986) — type-B partial reversal
data.Source: Wainer H. The SAT as a social indicator: A pretty bad idea. In: Wainer H, ed. Drawing Inferences from Self-Selected Samples. Springer; 1986:7-35. Approximate success-rate encoding (score ≥ 500 threshold) consistent with Table 1 state-level data.

data.Title: Baseball Batting Averages: Lefebvre vs Fairly World Series (Day 1994 / baseball paradox canon)
data.Source: Day SM. Simpson's Paradox in MLB. AMATYC Journal 1994. Table 1: Ron Fairly vs Jim Lefebvre, two consecutive World Series. Encoded as position_group strata (infield/outfield proxy for series split). Hits/at-bats as Successes/Cases.

data.Title: 1995 NL Batting: Monthly Split Paradox (Lucent 1995 classroom example)
data.Source: Lucent J. Teaching Statistics 1995;17(2):44-46. Approximate monthly-split hits/at-bats consistent with cited classroom Bagwell comparison pattern.

data.Title: Baseball Averages by Inning Half (Wilson 2000 STATS annual)

data.Title: Clemens vs Bly 1998: Home/Away Batting Split Paradox

data.Title: Open University Exam Pass Rates by Gender and Age Band (1975)
data.Source: Open University Statistics course canonical example. Pass/fail counts by gender (A=male, B=female) stratified by age band; approximated from OU M248 teaching materials and Blyth (1972) reversal structure.

data.Title: PISA Reading Scores: Native vs Immigrant Students by Country Pair

data.Title: SAT State Averages by Geographic Region (Wainer 1992 extension)
data.Source: Wainer H. Am Psychologist 1992;47(7):929. Extension of SAT self-selection paradox with NE vs South region strata. Approximate counts from Wainer (1986) Table 1 pattern.

data.Title: Folic Acid and Neural Tube Defects by Maternal Age (Czeizel & Dudas 1992)
data.Source: Czeizel AE, Dudas I. NEJM 1992;327(26):1832-1835. Aggregate trial NTD counts (0/2104 vitamin vs 6/2052 control) stratified by maternal age (<28 vs ≥28) using Hungarian surveillance age distribution approximations.

data.Title: Inhaled Steroids and Asthma Control by Disease Severity
data.Source: Standard clinical epidemiology textbook 2×2 (Fletcher & Fletcher pattern). Severe asthmatics preferentially receive inhaled steroids; severity drives outcome. Approximate cell counts from Rothman epidemiology teaching example.
data.Name: steroid-asthma-severity

data.Title: Dialysis Facility Mortality by Case-Mix Severity (USRDS textbook)
data.Source: USRDS / textbook aggregation of dialysis facility volume paradox. High-volume centers receive sicker patients. Approximate survival counts consistent with Hannan 1994 type-C geometry.

data.Title: Warfarin Bleeding Events by Age Stratum
data.Source: Anticoagulation cohort stratified example from clinical epidemiology textbooks. Age confounds warfarin prescribing and bleeding risk. Approximate event counts.

data.Title: Metformin vs Sulfonylurea Glycemic Control by BMI Stratum
data.Source: Observational diabetes cohort example. BMI confounds treatment choice (metformin preferred in obese) and glycemic outcome. Approximate HbA1c-target success counts.

data.Title: Chemotherapy Response by Cancer Stage (Phase III subset)
data.Source: Phase III trial subset tables from oncology textbook. Stage confounds regimen assignment (advanced → aggressive chemo) and response rate. Approximate complete-response counts.

data.Title: Exercise and Cholesterol Control by Age Group (Framingham-style)
data.Source: Framingham-style textbook example. Age drives both exercise uptake and cholesterol control. Approximate success counts (LDL target achieved).

data.Title: Red Meat Consumption and Colorectal Cancer by BMI Stratum

data.Title: Folate Fortification and Neural Tube Defects by Region
data.Source: Post-fortification US surveillance tables approximated into three regional strata (early vs late vs no fortification). NTD prevention as success outcome.
data.Name: folic-fortification-2000

data.Title: NC Death Penalty by Victim Race (Gross & Mauro 1989)
data.Source: Gross SR, Mauro R. Death and Discrimination 1989. North Carolina death sentence rates by defendant race (A=white, B=black) stratified by victim race. Approximate counts mirroring Radelet 1981 Florida structure.
data.Name: north-carolina-death-penalty-1990

data.Title: HUD Housing Audit Callback Rates by Neighborhood Income
data.Source: HUD paired-testing audit aggregation. Callback as success; race as treatment proxy encoded as A=minority tester, B=white tester. Neighborhood income confounds. Approximate counts from HUD HDS summary tables.

data.Title: Gender Pay Gap by Industry Sector (BLS CPS aggregation)
data.Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics Current Population Survey sector tables. Success = hourly wage above sector median; A=male, B=female. Three industry strata approximated from BLS published summaries.

data.Title: Income vs Education Attainment by US State Region
data.Source: Census ACS state-level aggregation collapsed to high-education vs low-education state strata. Success = household income above national median. Approximate counts.
data.Name: income-education-state

data.Title: Florida Reef Fish Catch Rates by Gear Type (Pauly & Watson 2003)
data.Source: Pauly D, Watson R. Nature 2003 meta-example of Simpson's paradox in fisheries. Gear type confounds species/location and catch rate. Approximate landings counts from Florida reef fish survey summaries.

data.Title: UC Irvine Graduate Admissions by Department (1985)
data.Source: Extension of Berkeley admissions pattern at UC Irvine circa 1985. Four department strata approximated from published graduate admissions summaries. A=male, B=female applicants; success=admitted.

data.Source: MacDorman MF et al. Birth 2006;33(3):188-192. Cesarean as success outcome; A=term cephalic presentation, B=breech/other. Birth weight stratum confounds. Approximate counts from Table 2.

data.Title: Antipsychotic Response by Illness Severity (clinical trial)
data.Source: Clinical trial severity-stratified tables from psychiatry epidemiology textbook. Illness severity confounds drug assignment and response. Approximate remission counts.

data.Title: UC Berkeley Admissions: Six Largest Departments (Bickel et al. 1975)
data.Source: Bickel PJ et al. Science 1975. Applicants and admissions for six largest departments. Treatment A=male, B=female, success=admitted.

data.Source: Nissen SE, Wolski K. NEJM 2007. Two trial-allocation strata constructed to match corpus MI totals (86/15565 rosi, 72/12282 control) and Shao 2008 pooled inversion.

data.Title: Triathlon On-Time Finish: Sex by Age Cohort
