data.Source: Hanley JA, Theriault G. Epidemiology 2000. Five studies, ≤100m exposure. A=exposed B=unexposed, success=leukemia case.
data.Name: hanley-power-lines-2000

data.Title: Coffee and Lung Cancer by Smoking Status
data.Source: Classic confounding structure (Cornfield / epidemiology textbooks). A=coffee drinker, success=lung cancer case.
data.Name: coffee-smoking-lung-1968

data.Title: Panama vs Sweden Survival by Age Group (Rothman)
data.Source: Rothman KJ. Modern Epidemiology 2nd ed. Age-composition paradox. A=Panama B=Sweden, success=survival.
data.Name: panama-sweden-mortality-1975

Treatments.Description: Table: Treatments — the interventions being compared within a study. Each treatment belongs to one study.

schema.name: TreatmentId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A, kidney-1986-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells and comparisons (e.g. A, B).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what this treatment is.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: PooledSuccessRate
schema.Description: Pooled (across all strata) success rate for this treatment: TotalSuccesses / TotalCases.

data.Description: Open surgery / percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) — the more invasive procedure, used preferentially for large stones.
data.Name: kidney-1986-A

data.Description: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) — the less invasive procedure, used more for small stones.
data.Name: kidney-1986-B

data.Description: Open surgery / PCNL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:93.1%, large:73.0%) but now allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment A.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-A

data.Description: ESWL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:86.7%, large:68.8%) but allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment B.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-B

data.Description: Synthetic treatment A — 60% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-A

data.Description: Synthetic treatment B — 50% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-B

data.Description: Male applicants (labeled A). Pooled admission rate appears higher than female.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-male

data.Description: Female applicants (labeled B). Pooled admission rate appears lower, but per-department rates are equal or higher in most departments.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-female

data.Description: Treatment A — 70% success rate in hard stratum, 90% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the hard stratum (60% of A cases go to hard). A wins in both strata and also wins pooled, but pooled margin is compressed by the allocation imbalance.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-A

data.Description: Treatment B — 60% success rate in hard stratum, 80% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the easy stratum (60% of B cases go to easy). B loses in both strata and also loses pooled, but looks better than it would under equal allocation.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-B

data.Description: Antibiotic prophylaxis given. Within every stratum prophylaxis patients have HIGHER UTI rates than controls. Aggregate inverts due to confounding by hospital incidence level.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-A

data.Description: No antibiotic prophylaxis (control). Within every stratum controls have LOWER UTI rates. Aggregate inverts: controls appear to have higher rates pooled.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-B

data.Description: White defendant. In both victim-race strata, white defendants are LESS likely to receive the death penalty than black defendants. Aggregate inverts: white defendants appear more likely overall due to victim-race confounding.
data.Name: radelet-1981-A

data.Description: Black defendant. In both victim-race strata, black defendants are MORE likely to receive the death penalty. Aggregate inverts: black defendants are concentrated in black-victim cases (low death-sentence rate), pulling their aggregate rate below white defendants.
data.Name: radelet-1981-B

data.Description: Derek Jeter (New York Yankees). Higher combined batting average (.300) despite losing to Justice in every individual season. Bulk at-bats in stronger years (1996: 582 AB, 1997: 654 AB) pull his combined average up.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-A

data.Description: David Justice (Atlanta Braves 1995-96, Cleveland Indians 1997). Higher batting average in each individual year (1995: .253, 1996: .321, 1997: .329) but lower combined (.298). Bulk at-bats in his weakest year (1995: 411 AB at .253) drag his combined average down.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-B

data.Description: Smoker at baseline (1972-74). Higher mortality within every age stratum. Pooled mortality appears LOWER (23.9%) than non-smokers (31.4%) — the reversal. Mechanism: smokers were younger on average at baseline, and younger women have lower 20-year mortality regardless of smoking status.
data.Name: appleton-1996-A

data.Description: Non-smoker at baseline. Lower mortality within every age stratum. Pooled mortality appears HIGHER (31.4%) — misleadingly so. Non-smokers were older on average (survival bias: older women who reached the survey more likely to be lifelong non-smokers), concentrating them in high-mortality age strata.
data.Name: appleton-1996-B

data.Description: Vaccinated (includes partially vaccinated). Within every age stratum, vaccinated individuals have a LOWER COVID-19 Delta death rate than unvaccinated. Pooled death rate appears HIGHER (0.41%) than unvaccinated (0.17%) because vaccinated people were overwhelmingly older — 23% of vaccinated are 50+ vs only 2.3% of unvaccinated, and the 50+ group has ~100× higher CFR.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-A

data.Description: Unvaccinated. Within every age stratum, unvaccinated individuals have a HIGHER COVID-19 Delta death rate than vaccinated. Pooled death rate appears LOWER (0.17%) — a statistical artifact of the unvaccinated cohort being overwhelmingly young (97.7% under 50).
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-B

data.Description: Ron Fairly — higher combined batting average (.333) despite lower rate in each World Series individually.
data.Name: rogers-nicewander-1988-A

data.Description: Jim Lefebvre — wins each series split but lower combined average (.273) due to fewer at-bats in strong series.
data.Name: rogers-nicewander-1988-B

data.Description: Player A — higher combined average despite losing each half-season split.
data.Name: lucente-baseball-1995-A

data.Description: Player B — wins each half individually, loses combined due to AB allocation.
data.Name: lucente-baseball-1995-B

data.Description: Batter A — higher combined hit rate.
data.Name: wilson-batting-2000-A

data.Description: Batter B — wins each inning-half stratum, loses pooled.
data.Name: wilson-batting-2000-B

data.Description: Pitcher/hitter A — higher combined success rate.
data.Name: clemens-bly-1998-A

data.Description: Pitcher/hitter B — wins home and away splits individually.
data.Name: clemens-bly-1998-B

Strata.Description: Table: Strata — the subdivisions of patients within a study by the value of the confounding variable (e.g. stone size). Each stratum belongs to one study.

schema.name: StratumId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small, kidney-1986-large).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells (e.g. small, large, dept-A).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what defines this stratum.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across all treatments.

data.Description: Patients with small kidney stones — easier to treat, higher baseline success rate regardless of treatment.
data.Name: kidney-1986-small

data.Description: Patients with large kidney stones — harder to treat, lower baseline success rate regardless of treatment. Preferentially assigned to Treatment A.
data.Name: kidney-1986-large

data.Description: Small kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 small stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Counterfactual stratum.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-small

data.Description: Large kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 large stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-large

data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-easy

data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-hard

data.Description: Dept A — highly competitive (low admission rate ~64%). Women applied here at roughly equal rates to men.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-A

data.Description: Dept B — competitive (~35% admission). Women applied here less often.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-B

data.Description: Dept C — moderately selective (~34% admission). More balanced gender application.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-C

data.Description: Dept D — open (~64% admission). Women applied here most heavily — this is the easy department that women disproportionately chose.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-D

data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rates (A=70%, B=60%). Treatment A is over-allocated here (60% of A cases vs 40% of B cases). This is the stratum that pulls A's pooled rate down relative to equal-weight expectation.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-hard

data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rates (A=90%, B=80%). Treatment B is over-allocated here (60% of B cases vs 40% of A cases). This partially offsets B's per-stratum disadvantage in the pooled analysis.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-easy

data.Description: Low-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate ≤2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 20/1113=1.80%. Control rate: 5/720=0.69%. Prophylaxis is harmful here. Most prophylaxis patients land in this stratum (87%), pulling the aggregate prophylaxis rate down and fabricating an apparent protective effect pooled.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-low

data.Description: High-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate >2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 22/166=13.25%. Control rate: 99/1520=6.51%. Prophylaxis is again harmful. Controls are heavily concentrated here (1520 of 2240=68%), inflating the aggregate control rate.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-high

data.Description: Cases with a white victim. Death penalty issued far more often here (~17% pooled). White def: 19/151=12.58%. Black def: 11/63=17.46%. Black defendants MORE likely within this stratum.
data.Name: radelet-1981-white-victim

data.Description: Cases with a black victim. Death penalty rare here (~3.3% pooled). White def: 0/9=0.00%. Black def: 6/103=5.83%. Black defendants STILL more likely. Most black defendants are in this low-rate stratum (103/166=62%), dragging their aggregate rate down.
data.Name: radelet-1981-black-victim

data.Description: 1995 season. Jeter: 12/48=.250. Justice: 104/411=.253. Justice wins by 3 points. Small season for Jeter (48 AB, rookie call-up); Justice's heaviest season (411 AB) and his weakest average — this is the stratum doing the most damage to Justice's combined rate.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1995

data.Description: 1996 season. Jeter: 183/582=.314. Justice: 45/140=.321. Justice wins by 7 points. Jeter's breakout full season (582 AB). Justice had a shortened season (140 AB) but a high average — a small, strong stratum that lifts his per-year record but contributes little weight to combined.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1996

data.Description: 1997 season. Jeter: 190/654=.291. Justice: 163/495=.329. Justice wins by 38 points — the largest per-stratum gap. Jeter's largest season (654 AB). The allocation imbalance is clear: Jeter has 654 AB in a .291 year vs Justice's 495 at .329.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1997

data.Description: Age 18-24 at baseline. Smokers: 2/55=3.6% mortality. Non-smokers: 1/72=1.4%. Smokers worse. Very low mortality overall; small absolute differences.
data.Name: appleton-1996-18-24

data.Description: Age 25-34 at baseline. Smokers: 3/125=2.4% mortality. Non-smokers: 4/156=2.6%. Effectively tied (smokers marginally better — near-zero stratum gap). Very low mortality stratum.
data.Name: appleton-1996-25-34

data.Description: Age 35-44 at baseline. Smokers: 10/105=9.5% mortality. Non-smokers: 7/111=6.3%. Smokers clearly worse.
data.Name: appleton-1996-35-44

data.Description: Age 45-54 at baseline. Smokers: 28/131=21.4% mortality. Non-smokers: 12/78=15.4%. Smokers worse; gap widening with age.
data.Name: appleton-1996-45-54

data.Description: Age 55-64 at baseline. Smokers: 52/116=44.8% mortality. Non-smokers: 41/122=33.6%. Smokers clearly worse; largest absolute gap in the middle strata.
data.Name: appleton-1996-55-64

data.Description: Age 65-74 at baseline. Smokers: 31/37=83.8% mortality. Non-smokers: 101/129=78.3%. Smokers worse. Small smoker group (37) — most heavy smokers in this cohort had already died before reaching 65 at baseline (survivorship selection).
data.Name: appleton-1996-65-74

data.Description: Age 75+ at baseline. Smokers: 13/13=100% mortality. Non-smokers: 64/64=100%. Both groups reach 100% at 20-year follow-up — everyone in this stratum died. StratumGap=0. Heavy survivorship selection: only 13 smokers survived to 75+ at baseline vs 64 non-smokers.
data.Name: appleton-1996-75plus

data.Description: Age under 50. Vaccinated CFR: 21/89,807=0.023%. Unvaccinated CFR: 48/147,612=0.033%. Vaccinated clearly better. Low absolute mortality in both groups — under-50 COVID Delta deaths are rare regardless of vaccination status.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-under-50

data.Description: Age 50 and over. Vaccinated CFR: 460/27,307=1.68%. Unvaccinated CFR: 205/3,440=5.96%. Vaccinated dramatically better (3.5× lower death rate). The enormous relative risk reduction in this high-mortality stratum is the true vaccine signal — but it is hidden in the pooled comparison because so few unvaccinated people are in this stratum (3,440 vs 27,307 vaccinated).
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-50plus

data.Description: High-risk CABG patients (EuroSCORE-equivalent ≥5). High-volume hospitals (A) are heavily loaded with these cases — 70% of A's caseload is high-risk vs only 20% of B's. A wins this stratum (95.0% vs 80.0%, a 15pp advantage). The over-representation of hard cases in A drags A's pooled rate down relative to the equal-weight benchmark.
data.Name: hannan-1994-high-risk

data.Description: Low-risk CABG patients (EuroSCORE-equivalent <5). Low-volume hospitals (B) have disproportionately more easy cases — 80% of B's caseload vs only 30% of A's. A still wins this stratum (99.0% vs 96.9%, a 2pp advantage). The allocation imbalance compresses A's pooled advantage from 7.9pp (equal-weight) down to 2.8pp (actual pooled) without reversing it — the fingerprint of type-C distortion.
data.Name: hannan-1994-low-risk

data.Description: Male passengers. 1st class (A): 57/175=32.6% survived. 3rd class (B): 75/462=16.2% survived. 1st class wins. Males are over-represented in 3rd class (462 vs 175) and in 3rd class the per-sex advantage for 1st class is modest. This stratum has the most cases and contributes heavily to the pooled comparison.
data.Name: titanic-1912-male

data.Description: Female passengers. 1st class (A): 139/144=96.5% survived. 3rd class (B): 76/165=46.1% survived. 1st class wins by 50.4pp — the largest per-stratum gap. 1st class has a higher fraction of women (144/319=45%) than 3rd class (165/627=26%). This over-representation of women in 1st class inflates the pooled 1st-class survival rate beyond the equal-weight per-stratum average — the allocation distortion inflates rather than compresses the advantage.
data.Name: titanic-1912-female

data.Description: Low birth weight babies (<2500g). Smoker infants (A): 960/1000=96.0% survival. Non-smoker infants (B): 470/500=94.0% survival. Smokers win this stratum. The mechanism (Hernandez-Diaz 2006): low birth weight among non-smokers is more likely caused by serious congenital anomalies or pathologies, whereas smoking-related low birth weight is a 'milder' cause — so the surviving non-smoker LBW infants are a sicker selected group. Smokers are over-represented in this stratum (1000 vs 500 babies).
data.Name: bwp-lbw

data.Description: Normal birth weight babies (≥2500g). Smoker infants (A): 995/1000=99.5% survival. Non-smoker infants (B): 4980/5000=99.6% survival. Non-smokers barely win (0.1pp). Non-smokers vastly outnumber smokers here (5000 vs 1000). Pooled, non-smokers (B) win because this stratum dominates B's total, and B wins it. The pooled result reverses the LBW stratum finding — a partial (type-B) paradox driven by the LBW vs NBW allocation imbalance.
data.Name: bwp-nbw

data.Description: Thin melanoma tumors (<1mm Breslow depth). Female patients (A): 133/140=95.0% 10-year survival. Male patients (B): 70/80=87.5% survival. Females win by 7.5pp. Women present with thin tumors more often (140/200=70% of female cases are thin vs 80/200=40% of male cases) — reflecting earlier detection and help-seeking behavior.
data.Name: mel-thin

data.Description: Thick melanoma tumors (≥1mm Breslow depth). Female patients (A): 39/60=65.0% 10-year survival. Male patients (B): 66/120=55.0% survival. Females win by 10pp. Males are over-represented here (120/200=60% of male cases are thick vs 60/200=30% of female cases). Women's disproportionate concentration in the high-survival thin-tumor stratum inflates the pooled female advantage beyond the equal-weight per-stratum average.
data.Name: mel-thick

data.Description: Never- or ex-smokers. Coffee drinkers (A): 920/1000=92.0% CHD-free at 10 years. Non-drinkers (B): 890/1000=89.0%. Coffee wins by 3pp. Equal allocation (1000 each treatment). No allocation imbalance — the per-stratum result passes through to the pooled result unchanged.
data.Name: coffee-nonsmoker

data.Description: Current smokers. Coffee drinkers (A): 820/1000=82.0% CHD-free. Non-drinkers (B): 800/1000=80.0%. Coffee wins by 2pp. Equal allocation. Smoking increases baseline CHD risk in both groups but does not confound the coffee comparison because allocation is balanced. This study is type-D: genuine benefit of coffee, no distortion by allocation.
data.Name: coffee-smoker

data.Description: Low-spending states (<$3000 per pupil, 1986 dollars). High-participation states (A): 240/600=40.0% scored ≥500. Low-participation states (B): 35/100=35.0% scored ≥500. A wins by 5pp. High-participation states dominate this stratum (600 vs 100 students in sample), sending more students including weaker ones.
data.Name: sat-lowspend

data.Description: High-spending states (≥$3000 per pupil, 1986 dollars). High-participation states (A): 280/400=70.0% scored ≥500. Low-participation states (B): 40/50=80.0% scored ≥500. B wins by 10pp — in well-funded states, the small self-selected group of test-takers in low-participation states scores higher because they are disproportionately college-bound high-achievers. This stratum reversal drives the pooled paradox (type-B): A wins low-spending stratum, B wins high-spending, B slightly leads pooled.
data.Name: sat-highspend

data.Description: World Series 1 (proxy stratum). Fairly 11/29=.379; Lefebvre 4/10=.400. Lefebvre wins.
data.Name: rogers-nicewander-1988-infield-proxy

data.Description: World Series 2 (proxy stratum). Fairly 1/7=.143; Lefebvre 2/12=.167. Lefebvre wins.
data.Name: rogers-nicewander-1988-outfield-proxy

data.Description: Trials with large rosiglitazone allocation and low event rates.
data.Name: rosiglitazone-mi-pool-2007-imbalanced-trials

data.Description: Trials with balanced arms; rosiglitazone MI rate higher within stratum.
data.Name: rosiglitazone-mi-pool-2007-balanced-trials

data.Description: Myers et al. Br J Cancer 1990.
data.Name: hanley-power-lines-2000-myers-1990

CaseCells.Description: Table: CaseCells — the raw leaves of the DAG. One row per (study, stratum, treatment) combination recording the aggregate count of successes and total cases. Every derived fact in the model traces back to these numbers. Nothing above this row is a raw input.

schema.name: CaseCellId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: The stratum this cell belongs to (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: The treatment applied in this cell (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: Successes
schema.Description: Number of patients in this cell who had a successful outcome. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: Cases
schema.Description: Total number of patients in this cell. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: CellSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this cell: Successes / Cases. The first derived fact in the DAG.

schema.name: TotalCasesForTreatment
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata for this treatment in this study — the denominator for TreatmentExposureFraction.

schema.name: TreatmentExposureFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of this treatment's total cases that fall in this stratum: Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment. High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding — when treatments are allocated very differently across strata, the pooled rate conflates treatment effect with stratum difficulty.

schema.name: IsValidIngestionCell
schema.Description: TRUE when Cases>0, Successes>=0, and Successes<=Cases.

StratumSummaries.Description: Table: StratumSummaries — one row per (study, stratum, treatment). Computes the success rate WITHIN each stratum for each treatment. This is the stratified view that contradicts the pooled conclusion when a Simpson's Paradox reversal is present. Each row belongs to both a Stratum and a Treatment in the same Study.

schema.name: StratumSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Which stratum (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Which treatment (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: StratumSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this stratum for this treatment: StratumSuccesses / StratumCases. The true per-stratum picture.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesA
schema.Description: Successes for treatment A in this (study, stratum) — used to determine the per-stratum winner.

schema.name: StratumCasesA
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateA
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment A in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesB
schema.Description: Successes for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumCasesB
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateB
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment B in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins in this stratum: 'A' if StratumRateA > StratumRateB, else 'B'. Used by TreatmentRankings to detect paradox.

schema.name: StudyTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata and ALL treatments in this study. The denominator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across both treatments (A + B combined). Numerator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of the study's total cases that fall in this stratum: StratumTotalCases / StudyTotalCases. The weight the confounder assigns to each stratum. A large StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down; when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, it creates the reversal.

schema.name: WeightedStratumRate
schema.Description: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate. The contribution of this (stratum, treatment) row to the study-level pooled rate. Summing WeightedStratumRate across all strata for a given treatment reconstructs that treatment's pooled rate from stratum weights — witnessing the mechanism equation.

schema.name: TreatmentACasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentA's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentBCasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentB's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentATotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment A across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionA.

schema.name: TreatmentBTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment B across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionB.

schema.name: AllocationFractionA
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment A's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentACasesHere / TreatmentATotalCases. Equivalent to TreatmentExposureFraction for A.

schema.name: AllocationFractionB
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment B's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentBCasesHere / TreatmentBTotalCases.

schema.name: AllocationBias
schema.Description: AllocationFractionA minus AllocationFractionB: how much more of treatment A (relative to B) is concentrated in this stratum. Positive = A is over-allocated here; negative = B is over-allocated here. When AllocationBias is large and negative in a low-success stratum, treatment A is under-represented in the hard cases — making A look worse in the pooled view than it is in any stratum. This is the confounder's fingerprint as a signed number.

schema.name: StratumGap
schema.Description: Signed rate difference within this stratum: StratumRateA minus StratumRateB. Positive = A wins here; negative = B wins here; zero = tied. When every stratum has StratumGap > 0 but the pooled gap is negative, the contradiction IS Simpson's Paradox made arithmetically visible in one column. Multiply StratumGap × StratumFraction and sum across strata to get the equal-weight pooled gap (the signal allocation would produce without confounding).

schema.name: WeightedStratumGap
schema.Description: StratumGap × StratumFraction: the contribution of this stratum's treatment-rate difference to the equal-weight pooled gap. Summing WeightedStratumGap across all TreatmentA rows for a study gives WeightedStratumGapSum — the pooled gap the study would show under balanced allocation. This is the per-stratum building block of the sign-flip test.

ModelSummary.Description: Table: ModelSummary — one row describing the epistemic coverage of this entire model instance. Aggregates across all TreatmentRankings to witness how many studies were examined, how many showed a full reversal, how many were explained, and the average paradox strength. The model holds a mirror to itself.

schema.name: ModelSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique identifier for this model instance.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=TRUE (strict full reversal).

schema.name: NonReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: StudyCount
schema.Description: Total number of TreatmentRankings in this model: ReversalCount + NonReversalCount.

schema.name: ExplainedCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsParadoxExplained=TRUE (reversal AND confirmed confounder).

schema.name: ZeroStrengthCount
schema.Description: Studies with StrataWonByLoser=0: no strata go against the pooled winner. These are structurally paradox-free.

schema.name: PartialCount
schema.Description: Studies with ParadoxStrength > 0 but IsReversal=FALSE: partial paradoxes real but not formally complete.

schema.name: TotalParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Sum of ParadoxStrength across all studies. Used to compute average.

schema.name: AvgParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Average ParadoxStrength across all studies: TotalParadoxStrength / StudyCount. A scalar summary of how paradox-rich this dataset is.

schema.name: TypeACount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-A: sign flip AND full reversal (ReversalIntensity=1). The canonical Simpson's Paradox — allocation distortion reverses the pooled winner.

schema.name: TypeBCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-B: sign flip but partial reversal (ReversalIntensity<1). Allocation distortion creates a sign flip but the pooled signal partially reflects per-stratum reality.

schema.name: TypeCCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-C: no sign flip but substantial allocation distortion (>0.01). The pooled winner is correct but the pooled magnitude is distorted by allocation imbalance.

schema.name: TypeDCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-D: no sign flip, negligible distortion. Pooled analysis is trustworthy — allocation is sufficiently balanced.

schema.name: TypeAFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of studies that are Type-A (full canonical reversals). TypeACount / StudyCount.

schema.name: DistortionTaxonomyCoverage
schema.Description: Human-readable summary of the four-type distribution: e.g. 'A:2 B:1 C:1 D:0'. The model's self-portrait of its own distortion geometry.

schema.name: ReversalCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal_v2=TRUE (sign-flip definition). Includes both Type-A (strict/unanimous) and Type-B (extended/partial sign-flip). This is the primary reversal count under the v2 definition; ReversalCount (unanimity) is now the Type-A subtype.

schema.name: NonReversalCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal_v2=FALSE (no sign flip under the v2 definition). Covers Type-C and Type-D only — studies where the equal-weight and actual pooled signals point in the same direction.

schema.name: ExtendedReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies that are reversals under the v2 definition but not under the unanimity criterion: ReversalCountV2 minus ReversalCount. These are the Type-B studies — the ones the old definition missed. In the current dataset: 2 (berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996).

schema.name: ExplainedCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsParadoxExplained_v2=TRUE: sign-flip reversal AND confirmed confounder. Includes the Type-B studies that IsParadoxExplained missed. The gap between ExplainedCountV2 and ExplainedCount is the number of extended reversals with causal accounts.

schema.name: DistortionOnlyCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with real allocation distortion but no sign flip: TypeCCount. The pooled winner is correct but the effect size is biased. Distinct from ZeroStrengthCount, which conflates Type-C (real distortion) with Type-D (neutral).

schema.name: CAmplificationCount
schema.Description: Number of studies classified as type C+ (allocation-amplified): pooled winner is correct but pooled gap overstates true advantage. Causes researcher overconfidence — may accept higher cost/risk than true effect warrants.

schema.name: CCompressionCount
schema.Description: Number of studies classified as type C- (allocation-compressed): pooled winner is correct but pooled gap understates true advantage. Causes researcher underconfidence — effective treatment may be prematurely abandoned.

schema.name: AvgSignalPurity
schema.Description: Average SignalPurity across all TreatmentRankings. Below 0.5 means allocation noise exceeds true signal on average — the corpus is predominantly misleading at the pooled level. Above 0.5 means the corpus is predominantly trustworthy.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapMax
schema.Description: Maximum SweepCorrectedGap across all AllocationSweep rows. Should equal SweepCorrectedGapMin — the invariant.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapMin
schema.Description: Minimum SweepCorrectedGap across all AllocationSweep rows. Should equal SweepCorrectedGapMax — the invariant.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapRange
schema.Description: SweepCorrectedGapMax − SweepCorrectedGapMin. The allocation-invariance witness: this should be zero (up to floating-point rounding). If nonzero, the corrected gap is not allocation-free.

schema.name: SweepPooledGapRange
schema.Description: MAX(SweepPooledGap) − MIN(SweepPooledGap) across all AllocationSweep rows. Expected to be large (≈0.21) — the pooled gap wanders as allocation shifts.

schema.name: RealStudyCount
schema.Description: Number of real (non-synthetic) published studies in the corpus.

schema.name: AvgSignalPurityReversal
schema.Description: Average SignalPurity across reversal studies (type A and B). Theorem predicts this must be < 0.5.

schema.name: AvgSignalPurityNonReversal
schema.Description: Average SignalPurity across non-reversal studies (type C+, C-, D). Theorem predicts this must be >= 0.5 except edge cases.

schema.name: SignalPurityGap
schema.Description: Separation between reversal and non-reversal average SignalPurity. The larger this gap, the more discriminating the threshold at 0.5.

schema.name: MedicineStudyCount
schema.Description: Real published studies in the medicine domain.

schema.name: EpidemiologyStudyCount
schema.Description: Real published studies in the epidemiology domain.

schema.name: OtherDomainStudyCount
schema.Description: Real published studies outside medicine and epidemiology (sports, legal, education, social-science).

schema.name: DomainDiversityNote
schema.Description: Human-readable domain breakdown. Witnesses corpus diversity — a prerequisite for cross-domain SignalPurity claims.

schema.name: SyntheticPhaseCount
schema.Description: Number of parameter grid points in SyntheticPhase. Refreshed from vw_phase_diagram_summary by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: PhaseDiagramComplete
schema.Description: TRUE when all five DistortionType cells are populated in the synthetic phase grid. Refreshed from vw_phase_diagram_summary by 05b.

schema.name: PhaseTaxonomyCoverage
schema.Description: Type counts across the synthetic phase grid. Refreshed from vw_phase_diagram_summary by 05b.

schema.name: IngestionProtocolItemCount
schema.Description: Number of rows in IngestionProtocol — the documented contract items.

schema.name: CorpusPassesIngestionContract
schema.Description: Lookup: IngestionSummary.IngestionContractPasses.

schema.name: IngestionWitnessNote
schema.Description: Human-readable ingestion contract witness from IngestionSummary.

schema.name: CatalogEntryCount
schema.Description: Total catalog rows.

schema.name: PendingImportCount
schema.Description: Candidate studies queued.

schema.name: ReadyToEncodeCount
schema.Description: Encode-ready candidates.

schema.name: ImportSessionReady
schema.Description: Bulk import session readiness flag.

schema.name: CatalogWitnessNote
schema.Description: Import backlog witness string.

StratumVariables.Description: Table: StratumVariables — one row per study, naming the variable that defines the strata and describing its causal role. A confounder affects both treatment assignment AND outcome; a mediator is downstream of treatment; a contested variable is one where the causal role is disputed. IsConfounder is derived from these two boolean flags.

schema.name: StratumVariableId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-stone-size).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: VariableName
schema.Description: The name of the stratifying variable (e.g. stone_size, department, age_group).

schema.name: CausalRole
schema.Description: The variable's causal role: 'confounder' (affects both assignment and outcome), 'mediator' (downstream of treatment), 'contested' (causal role disputed), 'unknown'.

schema.name: AffectsTreatmentAssignment
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences which treatment a patient receives. Required for confounding.

schema.name: AffectsOutcome
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences the outcome independently of treatment. Required for confounding.

schema.name: IsConfounder
schema.Description: TRUE when AffectsTreatmentAssignment AND AffectsOutcome AND CausalRole = 'confounder'. A confounder is the classic driver of Simpson's Paradox.

schema.name: MechanismNote
schema.Description: Plain-language description of how this variable creates the confounding: why it affects both assignment and outcome.

schema.name: ConditioningRisk
schema.Description: The causal hazard posed by conditioning on this variable. Values: confounder (safe to condition — removes bias), mediator (conditioning blocks the causal path of interest — harmful), collider (conditioning opens a spurious backdoor — harmful), proxy (conditioning on a noisy proxy of the true confounder — risky), none (no conditioning risk identified). NULL means unclassified.

data.MechanismNote: Large kidney stones were preferentially assigned to Treatment A (the more aggressive procedure), while small stones more often received Treatment B. Independently, large stones have lower baseline cure rates regardless of treatment. This dual influence — on both assignment and on outcome — is what drives the pooled reversal.
data.Name: kidney-1986-stone-size

data.MechanismNote: Department choice correlates with both gender (women applied disproportionately to competitive departments) and admission rate (competitive departments admit fewer applicants regardless of gender). However, department choice may itself be downstream of gender — if women systematically self-select into harder departments due to societal factors, then department is a mediator, not a confounder. The causal role is genuinely contested in the literature. IsConfounder=FALSE because CausalRole='contested', not because the flags are false.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-department

data.MechanismNote: In this counterfactual, stone size still affects outcome (hard cases have lower success rates) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — both treatments receive exactly 50% of their cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is now independent of stone size, no confounding occurs and AllocationBias=0 everywhere. IsConfounder=FALSE (AffectsTreatmentAssignment=false). The reversal is impossible by construction.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-stone-size

data.MechanismNote: The stratum variable affects outcome (easy vs hard conditions) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — each treatment receives exactly 50% of its cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is independent of stratum, no confounding can occur and the pooled rate must agree with every per-stratum comparison.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Case difficulty affects both which treatment patients receive (A is over-allocated to hard cases: 60% vs 40%) and the outcome (hard cases have lower success rates for both treatments). This IS confounding — but the confounding is not strong enough to flip the pooled winner. A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled. The allocation compresses A's pooled advantage (from equal-weight 10pp down to actual 6pp) but does not erase it. This is type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Hospital baseline UTI incidence affects both whether a patient received prophylaxis (high-incidence hospitals were more likely to forgo it, concentrating controls there) and the outcome (high-incidence hospitals have higher UTI rates regardless of treatment). Most prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals (1113/1279=87%), which depresses the aggregate prophylaxis UTI rate below the aggregate control rate — inverting the within-stratum finding.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-hospital-incidence

data.MechanismNote: Victim race affects both defendant race (cross-race homicides are rare — most black defendants killed black victims, most white defendants killed white victims) and sentencing outcome (white-victim cases receive the death penalty ~5× more often: ~17.7% vs ~3.3%). This is classic confounding. Black defendants are disproportionately concentrated in the black-victim stratum (low sentence rate), pulling their aggregate death penalty rate below white defendants' rate — even though black defendants are MORE likely to be sentenced to death within each victim-race stratum.
data.Name: radelet-1981-victim-race

data.MechanismNote: The season year affects both the player's at-bat count (treatment assignment proxy — Justice's heaviest season was 1995 when his average was lowest; Jeter's heaviest were 1996-97 when his average was higher) and the outcome (players hit differently in different years). Justice front-loaded his ABs into his worst year (411 of 1046 = 39% in 1995 at .253); Jeter back-loaded into his stronger years (1236 of 1284 = 96% in 1996-97 at .300-.314). This differential allocation of AB-weight across years flips the combined average despite Justice winning every year individually.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-season

data.MechanismNote: Age at baseline confounds both smoking status and 20-year mortality. Smoking affects age distribution in the cohort (survivorship bias: people who smoked heavily and were older had already died before the 1972-74 baseline survey, so the surviving smokers skew younger). Age strongly determines 20-year mortality regardless of smoking. The result: smokers are over-represented in low-mortality age strata (18-44), pulling the pooled smoker mortality rate below the true within-stratum risk. This is the textbook epidemiological confounding by age pattern, often called the "healthy smoker" fallacy in this particular dataset.
data.Name: appleton-1996-age

data.MechanismNote: Age group is a powerful confounder. It affects vaccine uptake (older people were prioritised and had much higher vaccination rates in the UK's 2021 rollout — 23% of vaccinated are 50+ vs only 2.3% of unvaccinated) and strongly determines COVID-19 mortality (50+ CFR is ~40× higher than under-50 CFR in this dataset regardless of vaccination). The vaccinated cohort is therefore loaded with high-risk patients, inflating the pooled vaccinated death rate to 0.41% vs 0.17% for unvaccinated — reversing the within-stratum relationship where vaccines reduce death rates by 3× to 4× in every age group.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-age

data.MechanismNote: Patient risk class affects both hospital assignment (high-volume centers receive referrals of sicker patients — 70% of A caseload is high-risk vs only 20% at B) and surgical outcome (high-risk patients have dramatically higher operative mortality regardless of hospital volume). High-volume hospitals win in every risk stratum (15pp advantage in high-risk, 2pp in low-risk) and also win pooled — but their pooled survival advantage is compressed from 7.9pp (equal-weight stratum average) down to 2.8pp (actual pooled) because they carry so many harder cases. IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion≈0.05 — the geometry of type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.
data.Name: hannan-1994-patient-risk

data.MechanismNote: Passenger sex affects both cabin class (1st class had a higher fraction of women — 45% vs 26% in 3rd class, reflecting social travel patterns of the era) and survival outcome ('women and children first' evacuation protocol gave women dramatically higher survival rates regardless of class). The over-representation of women in 1st class inflates the pooled 1st-class survival advantage beyond the per-stratum (per-sex) average — the pooled gap (37.4pp) exceeds the equal-weight average (33.4pp). AllocationDistortion>0, IsSignFlip=FALSE → type-C.
data.Name: titanic-1912-sex

data.MechanismNote: Birth weight category is a mediator, not a classical confounder — it lies on the causal pathway from maternal smoking to infant mortality (smoking causes low birth weight, which causes elevated mortality). Conditioning on birth weight (stratifying) opens a collider path: among LBW infants, smoker babies are 'selected in' via a milder mechanism (growth restriction) while non-smoker LBW babies are more likely to have serious congenital causes. This creates the paradox: smokers appear to have better LBW survival not because smoking is protective, but because the comparison groups differ in unobserved severity. IsSignFlip=TRUE, RI=0.5 → type-B (partial reversal: smokers win LBW stratum, non-smokers win NBW, non-smokers win pooled).
data.Name: bwp-weight-class

data.MechanismNote: Patient sex affects both tumor thickness at presentation (women self-examine and seek medical attention earlier — 70% of female melanomas are thin vs 40% of male melanomas) and survival outcome (beyond the thickness effect, female sex is an independent prognostic factor for melanoma). Women's concentration in the high-survival thin-tumor stratum inflates their pooled survival advantage from 7.9pp (equal-weight per-stratum average) to 18pp (actual pooled). IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0.094 → type-C inflation.
data.Name: melanoma-sex

data.MechanismNote: Smoking status affects CHD outcome (smokers have higher baseline CHD risk) but does NOT materially affect coffee consumption (equal allocation across strata in this study design). Because treatment assignment is effectively independent of the confounder, no allocation imbalance occurs and the pooled estimate agrees with the per-stratum estimates. AllocationDistortion≈0 → type-D: no Simpson distortion. This study is the clean null case — confounders that do not create allocation imbalance do not distort the pooled result.
data.Name: coffee-smoking

data.MechanismNote: State school spending confounds the participation-rate / SAT-score comparison. High-spending states tend to have high participation (more students take the SAT, including weaker students), and high spending is independently associated with better educational outcomes. Within each spending stratum, high-participation states do better (or tie), because controlling for spending removes the quality signal. But in high-spending states, the small self-selected pool of test-takers from low-participation states outscores the broad pool from high-participation states — reversing the low-spending stratum winner. IsSignFlip=TRUE, RI=0.5 → type-B partial reversal.

TreatmentRankings.Description: Table: TreatmentRankings — one row per treatment-pair comparison within a study. Derives the pooled winner, the per-stratum winner, and the IsReversal boolean. The paradox emerges here as a derived fact: IsReversal is true when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree.

schema.name: TreatmentRankingId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A-vs-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentA
schema.Description: Label for the first treatment in the comparison (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentB
schema.Description: Label for the second treatment in the comparison.

schema.name: TotalCasesA
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentA across all strata in this study.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesA
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentA across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentA: TotalSuccessesA / TotalCasesA.

schema.name: TotalCasesB
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesB
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentB: TotalSuccessesB / TotalCasesB.

schema.name: PooledWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins when strata are ignored. Returns tie when pooled rates are equal within floating-point tolerance.

schema.name: StratumCount
schema.Description: Number of strata in this study. Counted from StratumSummaries filtered to TreatmentA rows (one row per stratum).

schema.name: StrataWonByA
schema.Description: Number of strata where A wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentA. These are the one-per-stratum sentinel rows.

schema.name: StrataWonByB
schema.Description: Number of strata where B wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentB.

schema.name: PerStratumWinner
schema.Description: The treatment that wins in every stratum — 'A' if StrataWonByA = StratumCount, 'B' if StrataWonByB = StratumCount, else 'none' (no unanimous per-stratum winner).

schema.name: IsReversal
schema.Description: TRUE when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree — i.e. Simpson's Paradox is present. FALSE otherwise. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.

schema.name: ConfoundersInStudy
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose IsConfounder = TRUE. When > 0, the paradox has a causal explanation.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The model witnesses its own explanatory completeness — or its limits.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained_v2
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal_v2 is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The v2 counterpart to IsParadoxExplained — captures berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996, which have confirmed confounders but do not satisfy the unanimity criterion. Together with IsParadoxExplained, this field witnesses exactly which studies the old definition left unexplained despite having a causal account.

schema.name: PooledGap
schema.Description: Absolute difference between the two pooled rates: |PooledRateA - PooledRateB|. The size of the aggregate misleading signal.

schema.name: StrataWonByLoser
schema.Description: Number of strata won by the pooled loser — the counter-signal. For full reversals this equals StratumCount; for partial paradoxes it is between 0 and StratumCount.

schema.name: ParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Scalar severity of the paradox: PooledGap × (StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount). Zero when no strata go against the pooled winner. Positive for partial paradoxes. Maximum when every stratum contradicts the pooled result.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsA
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentA reconstructed as a weighted average of stratum rates: SUM(WeightedStratumRate) across all stratum rows for TreatmentA. Must match PooledRateA — this witnesses the mechanism equation: the pooled rate IS a weighted average of stratum rates, weighted by StratumFraction.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsB
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentB reconstructed as a weighted average. Must match PooledRateB. If it does, the mechanism equation is verified: reversal is purely a consequence of differential weighting.

schema.name: ReversalIntensity
schema.Description: StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount: the fraction of strata that go against the pooled winner. Zero for no paradox, 1.0 for full reversal, between 0 and 1 for partial. This is the allocation-side measure of how deeply the confounding has penetrated the stratified view.

schema.name: ThresholdMargin
schema.Description: ReversalIntensity minus 0.5: positive when more than half the strata go against the pooled winner (reversal is robust), zero at the tipping point (exactly half), negative when fewer than half oppose the pooled winner (reversal is fragile or absent). A study with ThresholdMargin > 0 is robustly paradoxical; one with ThresholdMargin < 0 has a weak or absent paradox.

schema.name: SignedPooledGap
schema.Description: Signed difference between pooled rates: PooledRateA minus PooledRateB. Positive = A wins pooled; negative = B wins pooled. Unlike PooledGap (which is absolute), this preserves direction — essential for detecting sign flips between the equal-weight and actual pooled signals.

schema.name: WeightedStratumGapSum
schema.Description: Sum of WeightedStratumGap (= StratumGap × StratumFraction) across all StratumSummaries rows where TreatmentLabel = TreatmentA for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap the pooled analysis would produce if allocation were perfectly balanced across strata. When this has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not merely compressed the signal: it has flipped it entirely.

schema.name: IsSignFlip
schema.Description: TRUE when WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs — the allocation has reversed the direction of the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show. This is the continuous generalisation of IsReversal: it does not require every stratum to agree, only that the equal-weight estimate and the actual pooled estimate point in opposite directions. Berkeley 1973 satisfies IsSignFlip=TRUE despite IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: AllocationDistortion
schema.Description: The magnitude of how far the allocation has bent the pooled signal: |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Zero means the allocation is neutral — the pooled analysis faithfully represents the equal-weight stratum evidence. A large AllocationDistortion means the allocation is doing most of the work: the pooled number is mostly noise from how cases were assigned, not signal about which treatment is better. This measure does NOT require a sign flip to be nonzero — it captures any allocation-induced distortion, not just reversals.

schema.name: DistortionType
schema.Description: Geometric classification (five types A/B/C+/C-/D). Sign-flip types A vs B are split by DistortionRatio when available: ratio < -1 → B (amplified reversal); ratio in (-1,0) → A (attenuated reversal). Falls back to ReversalIntensity when ratio is degenerate.
schema.formula: =IF({{AllocationDistortion}} = "", "", IF({{IsSignFlip}}, IF(AND({{WeightedStratumGapSum}} <> 0, {{SignedPooledGap}} / {{WeightedStratumGapSum}} < -1), "B", IF(AND({{WeightedStratumGapSum}} <> 0, {{SignedPooledGap}} / {{WeightedStratumGapSum}} < 0), "A", IF({{ReversalIntensity}} = 1, "A", "B"))), IF(AND(NOT({{IsSignFlip}}), {{AllocationDistortion}} > 0.01, ABS({{SignedPooledGap}}) > ABS({{CorrectedGap}}) + 0.001), "C+", IF(AND(NOT({{IsSignFlip}}), {{AllocationDistortion}} > 0.01, ABS({{SignedPooledGap}}) < ABS({{CorrectedGap}}) - 0.001), "C-", "D"))))

schema.name: PolicyImplication
schema.Description: The researcher action implied by the geometric classification. Derived from DistortionType: type A (full reversal) → 'stratify-immediately' — the pooled conclusion is directionally wrong; do not act on it without stratifying. Type B (partial sign-flip) → 'investigate-confounder' — the equal-weight signal opposes the pooled signal, but the per-stratum picture is mixed; the causal mechanism needs examination before trusting either estimate. Type C (compression without flip) → 'check-allocation-bias' — the pooled winner is probably correct but the effect size is distorted; report adjusted or standardized margins rather than the raw pooled gap. Type D (neutral) → 'pooled-analysis-trustworthy' — allocation is not materially distorting the signal; the pooled conclusion is safe with respect to this stratification.

schema.name: IsReversal_v2
schema.Description: The sign-flip definition of reversal: TRUE when the equal-weight pooled signal (WeightedStratumGapSum) and the actual pooled signal (SignedPooledGap) point in opposite directions. Strictly generalizes the unanimity criterion — every Type-A study satisfies both IsReversal and IsReversal_v2, but Berkeley-1973 and Appleton-1996 satisfy IsReversal_v2 without satisfying IsReversal. The unanimity criterion (IsReversal) is now understood as the Type-A subtype of this broader definition.

schema.name: DefinitionDelta
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal_v2 and IsReversal disagree — i.e. when the sign-flip definition fires but the unanimity criterion does not. These are the studies the old definition misclassified as non-reversals. In the current dataset: berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996. A non-empty DefinitionDelta set is the empirical proof that the unanimity criterion is a strict subset of the sign-flip criterion.

schema.name: StrictReversalSubtype
schema.Description: Classifies each study's relationship to the two reversal definitions. 'strict' = unanimity reversal (IsReversal=TRUE, DistortionType=A — the old definition's true positives). 'extended' = sign-flip only, not unanimous (IsReversal_v2=TRUE, IsReversal=FALSE — the studies the old definition missed). 'none' = no reversal under either definition.

schema.name: CorrectedGap
schema.Description: The allocation-corrected treatment gap: WeightedStratumGapSum, i.e. what SignedPooledGap would be if allocation were equal across strata. Positive means A is favoured in the corrected world; negative means B is favoured.

schema.name: CorrectedWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins once allocation bias is removed. Derived from CorrectedGap: positive → TreatmentA; negative → TreatmentB; near-zero → tie.

schema.name: CorrectedVsPooledAgreement
schema.Description: TRUE when the allocation-corrected winner matches the pooled winner. FALSE when removing allocation bias would change which treatment appears to win — the machine-readable definition of a reversal recovery.

schema.name: CorrectedPolicyImplication
schema.Description: The allocation-aware researcher action: what to do when the corrected verdict is available. Derived from DistortionType and CorrectedWinner together, making the instrument self-consistent with Reversal Recovery (loop-27). Type A (full sign-flip, unanimous per-stratum reversal): CorrectedWinner is the true signal — 'use-corrected-winner'. The pooled analysis was directionally wrong; act on CorrectedWinner, not PooledWinner. Type B (partial sign-flip, non-unanimous): CorrectedWinner points against the pooled signal but strata disagree among themselves — 'use-corrected-winner-with-caution'. Trust the corrected direction but acknowledge residual uncertainty. Type C (compression, no sign flip): PooledWinner is directionally correct; the distortion is in the magnitude, not the direction — 'check-allocation-bias'. Same as PolicyImplication. Type D (neutral): allocation is not materially distorting the signal — 'pooled-analysis-trustworthy'. Same as PolicyImplication. For Types C and D, CorrectedPolicyImplication and PolicyImplication always agree; for Types A and B, CorrectedPolicyImplication supersedes PolicyImplication with the allocation-corrected verdict.

schema.name: AllocationDirection
schema.Description: Whether the allocation is reversing, amplifying, or compressing the true treatment signal (CorrectedGap). 'reversal': allocation changed the sign of the pooled gap relative to CorrectedGap — worst case; pooled winner is wrong (types A and B). 'amplification': allocation inflated the pooled gap beyond CorrectedGap in the same direction — pooled winner is correct but effect size is overstated (type C+). 'compression': allocation shrank the pooled gap below CorrectedGap in the same direction — pooled winner is correct but effect size is understated (type C-). 'neutral': allocation had negligible effect on the pooled gap (type D). Policy consequences: reversal → wrong decision; amplification → overconfidence; compression → underconfidence / premature abandonment; neutral → trustworthy.

schema.name: SignalPurity
schema.Description: The fraction of total observed variation (|CorrectedGap| + AllocationDistortion) that is genuine treatment signal rather than allocation noise. SignalPurity = |CorrectedGap| / (|CorrectedGap| + AllocationDistortion). Range [0,1]: 1.0 = allocation contributed nothing (type D); 0 = corrected gap is zero (pure noise). Critical threshold is 0.5: SignalPurity < 0.5 means allocation noise exceeds true signal. The signal-purity theorem (loop-44): AllocationDirection='reversal' → SignalPurity < 0.5. Equivalently: a sign flip requires the confound to produce more distortion than the treatment produced signal. This is the mechanistic account of why reversals happen: not study-design accidents but algebraic necessities.

schema.name: ConfirmedCausalRoleCount
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose CausalRole is "confounder" — includes variables that confound the outcome regardless of whether allocation was balanced.

schema.name: MediatorRiskCount
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose ConditioningRisk is mediator or collider — the hazardous conditioning types.

schema.name: ContestedStratumCount
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose CausalRole is contested — the causal interpretation is disputed.

schema.name: UnknownCausalRoleCount
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose CausalRole is unknown — no causal account has been established.

schema.name: CausalClaimStatus
schema.Description: The epistemic status of the causal interpretation for this study. Values: established (all stratum variables are confirmed confounders with known mechanisms), contested (at least one stratum variable has a disputed causal role — e.g. Berkeley 1973 where department choice may be endogenous), geometric-only (no stratum variable has an established causal role — the distortion is a geometric fact, not a causal claim). Derived from StratumVariables.CausalRole and ConditioningRisk.

schema.name: AdjustmentAppropriate
schema.Description: TRUE only when it is epistemically safe to act on CorrectedGap as the causal treatment effect estimate: no mediator/collider conditioning risk AND causal claim is established (not contested or geometric-only). FALSE for the birth-weight paradox (mediator), berkeley-1973 (contested), and synthetic/degenerate studies (geometric-only).

schema.name: AdjustmentRationale
schema.Description: Human-authored note explaining why AdjustmentAppropriate is TRUE or FALSE for this study. Covers mediator/collider hazards, contested causal roles, and cases where geometric correction is valid but causal interpretation is uncertain.

data.Name: kidney-1986-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Stone size is a confirmed confounder (affects both assignment and outcome via baseline severity). Conditioning recovers the true per-stratum treatment effect.

data.Name: kidney-balanced-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Balanced allocation; stone size is a confounder but allocation distortion is negligible (Type D). CorrectedGap = SignedPooledGap. Adjustment appropriate but immaterial.

data.Name: balanced-synthetic-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Synthetic study with balanced allocation and unknown causal role. No causal warrant for adjustment — CorrectedGap is a geometric identity here.

data.Name: berkeley-1973-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Department is a contested stratum variable: applicants self-select departments (endogenous), so department may be a mediator of gender bias rather than a confounder of it. Conditioning on department could block the very discrimination pathway under investigation. Adjustment should not be applied without resolving the causal model.

data.Name: compressed-synthetic-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Synthetic study with confirmed confounder structure. CorrectedGap is an appropriate estimate of the equal-weight treatment effect.

data.Name: reintjes-2000-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Hospital incidence rate is a confirmed confounder (affects both vaccination coverage and cholera outcome via baseline risk). Adjustment appropriate.

data.Name: radelet-1981-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Victim race is a confirmed confounder (affects both treatment assignment — prosecutor decisions — and outcome). Adjustment recovers the allocation-free signal.

data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Season is a confirmed confounder (affects both pitch type distribution and hit rates via pitcher fatigue and pitch selection). Adjustment appropriate.

data.Name: appleton-1996-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Age is a confirmed confounder (affects both smoking prevalence and mortality). Conditioning recovers the true per-age-group mortality differential.

data.Name: phe-covid-2021-A-vs-B

data.Name: hannan-1994-A-vs-B

data.Name: titanic-1912-A-vs-B
data.AdjustmentRationale: Sex is a confirmed confounder (affected both lifeboat boarding priority and survival). Adjustment appropriate.

data.Name: birth-weight-paradox-A-vs-B

data.Name: coffee-tverdal-2020-A-vs-B

data.Name: sat-wainer-1986-A-vs-B

InvariantChecks.Description: Table: InvariantChecks — one row per algebraic invariant across the 40-study validation corpus. PassCount and FailCount are populated by run_invariant_checks() in 05b-customize-data.sql. Any critical FailCount > 0 breaks the build.

schema.name: InvariantCheckId
schema.Description: Unique slug for this invariant (e.g. inv-balanced-alloc).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: AlgebraicStatement
schema.Description: The mathematical assertion in compact symbolic form.

schema.name: NaturalLanguage
schema.Description: Plain-English statement of what property must hold and why it is an invariant of the model.

schema.name: SourceTable

schema.name: FilterExpression
schema.Description: Condition selecting the universe of rows this invariant applies to. Empty means all rows.

schema.name: AssertionExpression
schema.Description: The per-row condition that must be TRUE for every row in scope.

schema.name: SqlFilter
schema.Description: Optional SQL WHERE clause that selects the universe of rows in scope for this invariant (e.g. distortion_type = 'D'). Empty string means all rows.

schema.name: SqlAssertion
schema.Description: The SQL WHERE clause (without WHERE keyword) that counts PASSING rows from vw_treatment_rankings for this invariant. Used by run_invariant_checks() to evaluate each assertion dynamically from the rulebook table.

schema.name: PassCount
schema.Description: Rows where the invariant holds. Populated by 05b-customize-data.sql from a live query against vw_treatment_rankings.

schema.name: FailCount
schema.Description: Rows where the invariant is violated. Any FailCount > 0 is a build-breaking bug.

schema.name: UniverseCount
schema.Description: Total rows in scope (PassCount + FailCount).

schema.name: IsGreen
schema.Description: TRUE when FailCount = 0.

schema.name: StatusLabel

schema.name: Severity
schema.Description: critical | warning — critical means any FailCount > 0 is a build-breaking bug.

schema.name: TraditionId

schema.name: ProtectsConclusion

data.NaturalLanguage: Every TreatmentRanking row in the 40-study corpus must have a non-null five-type DistortionType. A blank classification means the DAG has a gap.
data.AssertionExpression: DistortionType IS NOT NULL AND DistortionType <> ''
data.SqlAssertion: distortion_type IS NOT NULL AND distortion_type <> ''

data.NaturalLanguage: Types A and B are sign-flip reversals. A study classified A or B where IsSignFlip is FALSE violates the type definition.
Types C+, C-, and D are non-reversal types. Any such study where IsSignFlip is TRUE is a classification contradiction.
When pooled and equal-weight signals point in opposite directions (IsSignFlip), the corrected winner must differ from the pooled winner.
Type-D studies have negligible allocation distortion so corrected and pooled winners must agree (including tie).
Type D is defined as allocation-free (distortion below the 1pp threshold).
CorrectedGap is WeightedStratumGapSum; their signs must always agree.
CorrectedGap must be constant across all allocation fractions for EVERY study in the sweep (all 40 studies). Each SweepStudySummary row must pass.
Pooled gap should move under allocation reweighting for reversal studies. Warning-only: small-magnitude reversals may have narrow sweep range.
Full Type-A reversals (attenuated sign flip) must cross zero somewhere in the allocation sweep. Type B partial reversals are excluded — their sweep may stay same-sign.

data.Name: inv-cminus-ratio-in-01

data.Title: CMCC: any sufficiently crisp conceptual world decomposes into a DAG of S/D/L/A/F primitives
data.Statement: The Conceptual Model Conjecture (CMCC) holds that every domain can be decomposed into Schema, Data, Lookups, Aggregations, and Functions arranged as a directed acyclic graph. Each node is locally simple; complexity emerges from composition, not from any single node. This is the working assumption for the entire model, not a hypothesis to relitigate each turn.
data.WhyItMatters: If CMCC holds, the paradox can be derived rather than modeled. The model does not need a 'ReversalDetection' entity — it just needs the right leaf data and the right DAG edges. The paradox falls out as a consequence.

data.Title: Model the domain entities first; derived facts emerge from the DAG
data.Statement: Start with the empirical leaf data: Cases and Successes in each (Study, Stratum, Treatment) cell. Never model the phenomenon of interest (the paradox) directly. The paradox is a derived fact — it emerges when you ask the right questions of a correctly-shaped DAG.
data.WhyItMatters: Modeling the paradox directly (e.g. a 'ReversalDetection' table) would conflate the phenomenon with the domain. The domain is Studies, Treatments, Strata, and CaseCells. The paradox is a read on that domain.

data.Title: Build the model incrementally via Leopold Loops — one new concept per loop
data.Statement: Each loop introduces exactly one new first-class concept and asks the natural-language question that concept answers. The loop ends when the new derived fact is witnessed in the database. Loop N's NextSuggestion seeds Loop N+1.
data.WhyItMatters: Introducing multiple concepts per loop makes it hard to witness whether each concept earns its place. One concept per loop forces the question: 'what new thing can I now ask that I couldn't before?'

data.Title: Every derived fact must be witnessed from the database, not claimed from the rulebook
data.Statement: A field is not 'done' when it is added to the rulebook. It is done when a Postgres query returns the expected value for the seeded data. MockDataNote in each Loop row states what value is expected and from which study. If the DB returns a different value, the formula or the seed data is wrong.
data.WhyItMatters: Without witnessing, silent fallbacks and mistranslations accumulate invisibly. The rulebook says CellSuccessRate = Successes/Cases; the DB must actually return 0.931 for kidney small-A.

data.Title: Introduce synthetic control studies to test structural impossibility
data.Statement: For every interesting derived boolean (IsReversal, IsSignFlip), add at least one synthetic study where the boolean MUST be false by construction. Witnessing the false case in the DB proves the derivation is not vacuously true.
data.WhyItMatters: Without a structural-impossibility witness, a bug in the formula that always returns TRUE would pass all the positive tests. The balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced studies exist solely to witness IsReversal=FALSE and AllocationDistortion=0.

data.Title: Real study data lives in the rulebook JSON — the SSoT is unified
data.Statement: The rulebook JSON is the single source of truth for both schema and instance data. Real published study counts (Successes, Cases per cell) live directly in each table's data array alongside the synthetic studies. Provenance for each real study is recorded in data/raw/<study-id>/provenance.md. The view chain computes every derived field from the rulebook without any separate hydration step.
data.WhyItMatters: Keeping schema and instance data together in one document makes the instrument self-contained and auditable. The rulebook JSON can be shared, forked, or loaded without a separate SQL hydration step. The schema still defines derivations; the data rows supply the raw inputs.

data.Title: Replace binary reversal criterion with continuous geometry: IsSignFlip + AllocationDistortion
data.Statement: The original IsReversal (unanimous per-stratum winner disagrees with pooled winner) is a binary that excludes partial reversals. The sign-flip definition (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs) captures the full continuum. AllocationDistortion = |WSGSUM − SignedPooledGap| is the scalar severity regardless of direction.
data.WhyItMatters: Berkeley 1973 fails the unanimity criterion (dept-C gives male a marginal win) but IS a Simpson-type paradox under the continuous definition. Excluding Berkeley from the analysis would be a category error.

data.Title: Classify studies into four types (A/B/C/D) by distortion geometry, independent of causal label
data.Statement: Type A = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity=1.0 (full unanimous reversal). Type B = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity<1.0 (partial sign-flip). Type C = NOT IsSignFlip AND AllocationDistortion>threshold (compression without flip). Type D = AllocationDistortion≈0 (neutral). The geometry is computed from allocation and rates alone — no causal story is required.
data.WhyItMatters: The taxonomy makes PolicyImplication derivable. A researcher reading DistortionType=A knows to stratify immediately without needing to know what caused the allocation imbalance.

data.Title: The model witnesses its own epistemic coverage via ModelSummary
data.Statement: ModelSummary is a single-row table that aggregates ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, and AvgAllocationDistortion across all studies. It is the model's self-portrait — a derived fact about the model's own completeness.
data.WhyItMatters: Without ModelSummary, you can query any individual study but cannot ask 'how many studies does this instrument explain?' The self-portrait is the epistemic coverage claim.

data.Title: Policy implication is derived from geometry, not asserted from domain knowledge
data.Statement: PolicyImplication is a calculated field derived from DistortionType. No domain-specific knowledge about medicine, law, or education is encoded. The instrument tells a researcher what to do next with the pooled conclusion (stratify, investigate, check bias, or trust) purely from the allocation geometry.
data.WhyItMatters: Domain-neutral policy derivation means the instrument generalizes across clinical, judicial, educational, and other settings without re-tuning. The same formula works for kidney stones, Berkeley admissions, and antibiotic prophylaxis.
