data.Title: Simpson's Paradox is a derived fact from the DAG, not a modeled entity
data.Evidence: IsReversal (and later IsSignFlip) falls out as a calculated field on TreatmentRankings, derived from aggregated CaseCell counts. No 'ReversalDetection' entity was ever needed.

data.Title: The reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an allocation artifact — same rates with balanced allocation produce no reversal
data.Evidence: kidney-balanced uses identical stratum success rates to kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0. The paradox is algebraically isolated as a property of allocation, not of treatment efficacy.

data.Title: The unanimity criterion (IsReversal) incorrectly excludes Berkeley 1973 despite its being a Simpson-type paradox
data.Evidence: Berkeley: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.1934 — greater distortion than kidney-1986. But IsReversal=FALSE because dept-C marginally favors male. The unanimity criterion is a strict subtype, not the primary definition.

data.Title: Allocation can compress the pooled signal without flipping it (type-C): a class invisible to IsReversal and IsSignFlip but visible to AllocationDistortion
data.Evidence: compressed-synthetic: A wins every stratum and wins pooled, but the pooled margin is 6pp rather than the equal-weight 10pp. AllocationDistortion=0.0400, IsSignFlip=FALSE. The pooled winner is correct; the effect size is wrong by 4pp.

data.Title: Two real published studies (reintjes-2000, radelet-1981) hydrate as type-A full reversals without schema changes
data.Evidence: Both studies entered via 05b-customize-data.sql. Witnessed: reintjes AllocationDistortion=0.0516, DistortionType=A; radelet AllocationDistortion=0.0684, DistortionType=A. No DAG or schema change was needed — the hydration contract generalizes.

data.Title: PolicyImplication is domain-neutral: the same formula produces researcher actions across medicine, law, and education
data.Evidence: All five current studies (kidney-1986, berkeley-1973, compressed-synthetic, kidney-balanced, balanced-synthetic) plus two real studies produce unambiguous PolicyImplication values from DistortionType alone. No domain-specific knowledge is encoded in the formula.

data.Title: ModelSummary witnesses the instrument's own epistemic coverage as a first-class derived fact
data.Evidence: ModelSummary aggregates ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeA/B/C/D counts, and AvgAllocationDistortion across all studies. The model describes its own completeness in a single row.

data.Title: Does the published literature contain real type-C studies (compression without sign flip)?
data.Evidence: Witnessed in loop-24: hannan-1994 (CABG surgery), titanic-1912, melanoma-altman-1991, and coffee-tverdal-2020 all hydrate as DistortionType=C. AllocationDistortion>0.01, IsSignFlip=FALSE — the correct pooled direction but compressed magnitude. The type-C cell is not a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: AllocationDistortion is a geometric measure; causal interpretation requires external domain knowledge, gated by AdjustmentAppropriate
data.Evidence: Berkeley has the highest AllocationDistortion (0.1934) but CausalRole=contested (mediator vs confounder), proving that geometric severity and causal confounding are not the same thing. The instrument is deliberately geometric: it classifies distortion type and flags the corrected winner from allocation arithmetic alone. Whether it is safe to act on that correction as a causal claim is answered by AdjustmentAppropriate — which gates on ConditioningRisk and CausalClaimStatus. The two layers are complementary, not competing. The instrument computes the geometry; the researcher supplies the causal account. This is a scope boundary, not a limitation.

data.Title: The full instrument can be specified precisely enough for any researcher to apply it to a new study
data.Evidence: Witnessed in loop-26: InstrumentSpec table enumerates 5 input fields, 10 derived coordinates, 2 classification rules, and 3 adapter-requirement rows. Machine-verifiable against all 16 studies in the validation set.

data.Title: The model can produce the allocation-corrected winner directly from existing DAG fields — no new data required
data.Evidence: CorrectedGap = WeightedStratumGapSum (already derived in loop-12/17). CorrectedWinner is a sign-read on CorrectedGap. CorrectedVsPooledAgreement is TRUE for Type-D studies and FALSE for Type-A/B studies. For kidney-1986: CorrectedGap=+0.0537, CorrectedWinner=A, CorrectedVsPooledAgreement=FALSE (pooled said B; corrected says A). The reversal recovery is free — it costs zero new data.

data.Name: conc-12-signal-purity-theorem
data.Title: SignalPurity < 0.5 is a necessary condition for allocation-driven reversal
data.Evidence: Witnessed across all 9 reversal studies (type A + B) in the 16-study corpus: every study with AllocationDirection='reversal' has SignalPurity < 0.5. The algebraic proof: reversal requires SignedPooledGap and CorrectedGap to have opposite signs, which requires AllocationDistortion > |CorrectedGap|, which is exactly SignalPurity < 0.5. inv-signal-purity-sign-flip enforces this as a DAG invariant. AvgSignalPurityReversal ≈ 0.35 vs AvgSignalPurityNonReversal ≈ 0.65 — gap of ~0.30.

data.Name: conc-13-cplus-cminus-opposite-errors
data.Title: C+ (amplification) and C- (compression) are epistemically opposite errors with opposite clinical implications
data.Evidence: titanic-1912 (C+, DistortionRatio=1.359): allocation inflates first-class survival advantage from 27.5pp corrected to 37.4pp pooled — overconfidence. melanoma-altman-1991 (C+, DistortionRatio=2.087): allocation doubles apparent female survival advantage — severe overconfidence. hannan-1994 (C-, DistortionRatio=0.314): allocation compresses CABG advantage from 8.9pp corrected to 2.8pp pooled — underconfidence, risk of premature treatment abandonment. The prior type-C category hid this distinction. Now separated by DistortionRatio > 1 (C+) vs DistortionRatio ∈ (0,1) (C-).

data.Name: conc-14-scale-is-discovery-path
data.Title: Cross-domain SignalPurity correlations require a pre-registered unbiased corpus of 50+ studies to distinguish pattern from selection artifact
data.Evidence: Current 16-study corpus: 3 synthetic, 13 real. Domain breakdown: medicine=3, epidemiology=6, other=4. The instrument can classify any 2xK contingency table in five inputs. But detecting whether Domain predicts DistortionType, or whether SampleSize correlates with SignalPurity, requires enough studies per cell to reach statistical power. Minimum target: 8 studies per domain × 5 domains = 40+, ideally 50-80. Pre-registration of inclusion criteria (any real published 2xK study with a named confounder) prevents cherry-picking. This is the only path from clean instrumentation to empirical discovery.

data.Title: Strata Detail
data.Purpose: Show every (stratum, treatment) cell for a study: StratumSuccessRate, AllocationBias, StratumGap, WeightedStratumGap — the leaf-level evidence for why the pooled conclusion is or isn't misleading.

data.Title: Model Summary

data.Title: Loop History
data.Purpose: Show the full build history: all 31 loops with their status, new concept, domain question, and mock data note. Lets a developer understand why the model is shaped the way it is — the DAG is the story.

data.Title: Methodology
data.Purpose: Show the ten first-principles methodological axioms that shaped this model — from CMCC as the floor, through DAG-first decomposition, to the four-type taxonomy. Enables a developer to apply the same process to a new domain.
data.UserAction: Click IllustratedByLoop to jump to that loop in the Loop History screen.

data.Title: Conclusions
data.Purpose: Show all witnessed and planned conclusions, grouped by category (domain / instrument / methodology / open-question). The read-out of what this model has actually established.
data.KeyFields: Category, Status, Title, Evidence, WitnessedInLoop, TargetLoop
data.UserAction: Filter by Status (witnessed/planned/open); click WitnessedInLoop to navigate to Loop History.

UIComponents.Description: Table: UIComponents — the reusable visual building blocks used across UIScreens. Each row defines one component: what it renders, which entity it reads, and what props it requires. A developer can implement the full UI by composing these components onto the screens defined in UIScreens.

schema.name: ComponentId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. comp-01-distortion-badge).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors ComponentId.

schema.name: ComponentName
schema.Description: Code-level component name (PascalCase, e.g. DistortionBadge).

schema.name: UsedOnScreens
schema.Description: Comma-separated ScreenIds where this component appears.

schema.name: PrimaryEntity
schema.Description: The rulebook entity this component reads (the vw_* view).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: What this component renders and what decisions a developer must make to implement it.

schema.name: RequiredFields
schema.Description: Comma-separated rulebook fields this component must receive as props.

schema.name: VisualSpec
schema.Description: Plain-English description of the visual output: shape, color coding, interactivity.

data.Description: A colored pill/chip that shows DistortionType (A/B/C/D) and PolicyImplication for one study. Color-coded: A=red (stratify-immediately), B=orange (investigate-confounder), C=yellow (check-allocation-bias), D=green (pooled-analysis-trustworthy).
data.VisualSpec: Pill shape, 2–3 chars for the type letter, tooltip on hover showing PolicyImplication text. Consistent color across all screens.

data.Description: A side-by-side horizontal bar chart comparing PooledRateA vs PooledRateB (or StratumRateA vs StratumRateB for strata). The bar for the loser under equal-weight allocation is visually distinguished from the bar for the pooled loser.
data.RequiredFields: PooledRateA, PooledRateB, PooledWinner, WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap, IsSignFlip
data.VisualSpec: Two horizontal bars, labeled A and B. Arrow or bracket showing the gap. IsSignFlip=TRUE triggers a 'reversal' annotation. Width proportional to rate (0–1 scale).

data.Description: A grid of (Stratum × Treatment) cells where cell color encodes AllocationBias magnitude and sign. Positive bias (A over-allocated here) in one color; negative (B over-allocated) in another. Cell text shows AllocationFractionA and AllocationFractionB.
data.RequiredFields: StratumLabel, TreatmentLabel, AllocationBias, AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, StratumSuccessRate
data.VisualSpec: Matrix layout: rows=strata, cols=treatments. Diverging color scale centered at 0. Tooltip shows raw Cases and Successes from CaseCells.

data.Description: A 2D scatter plot with AllocationDistortion on the x-axis and ReversalIntensity on the y-axis. Each point is one study, colored by DistortionType. Quadrant lines at AllocationDistortion=0.01 (threshold) and ReversalIntensity=1.0 (unanimity) divide the plane into the four types.
data.VisualSpec: SVG or canvas scatter. Labeled quadrant regions. Clickable points. Legend showing type → color → policy action.

data.Description: A waterfall chart showing how each stratum's WeightedStratumGap contributes to WeightedStratumGapSum, then how the allocation re-weights them to produce SignedPooledGap. The visual makes the mechanism equation explicit: pooled = Σ(weight × rate).
data.RequiredFields: StratumLabel, StratumGap, StratumFraction, WeightedStratumGap, WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap
data.VisualSpec: Waterfall/stacked bar: each stratum's WeightedStratumGap as a segment; running sum shown; final total vs actual SignedPooledGap shown side-by-side. IsSignFlip highlights the sign difference.

data.Description: A summary scorecard showing all ModelSummary fields as labeled metric tiles: StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeA/B/C/D counts, AvgAllocationDistortion, TotalParadoxStrength.
data.RequiredFields: StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, ZeroStrengthCount, TotalParadoxStrength, AvgParadoxStrength
data.VisualSpec: Grid of metric tiles. Each tile: large number, small label. Color: ReversalCount in orange/red; ExplainedCount in green; TypeD in grey. Single row layout on desktop, 2-column on mobile.

data.Description: A card showing one StratumVariable row: VariableName, CausalRole badge, AffectsTreatmentAssignment checkbox, AffectsOutcome checkbox, IsConfounder result, and MechanismNote prose block.
data.RequiredFields: VariableName, CausalRole, AffectsTreatmentAssignment, AffectsOutcome, IsConfounder, MechanismNote
data.VisualSpec: Card with header=VariableName, CausalRole as a badge (confounder=red, mediator=orange, contested=yellow, unknown=grey), two boolean indicators, and MechanismNote as body text.

data.Description: A vertical timeline of all Loops rows, grouped by Status. Each entry shows LoopId, Title, Status indicator, NewConcept chip, and an expandable section with DomainQuestion, MockDataNote, and NextSuggestion.
data.RequiredFields: LoopId, Title, Status, NewConcept, DomainQuestion, MockDataNote, NextSuggestion
data.VisualSpec: Vertical list. Status=complete: solid circle; in-progress: half-filled; planned: open circle. Expandable accordion per entry. Color: complete=green, in-progress=blue, planned=grey.

data.Description: A prominent banner at the top of the Study Detail screen showing PolicyImplication as a clear action statement, color-coded by DistortionType. Includes a one-sentence explanation of why this action follows from the geometry.
data.VisualSpec: Full-width colored banner. Large icon (e.g. warning triangle for A, magnifying glass for B, scale for C, checkmark for D). Bold action text. Secondary line explaining the geometric basis.

data.Description: A filterable list of Conclusions rows grouped by Category and Status. Each entry shows the Title, Status badge, Evidence summary, and a link to the WitnessedInLoop or TargetLoop.
data.RequiredFields: Category, Status, Title, Evidence, WitnessedInLoop, TargetLoop
data.VisualSpec: Grouped list with category headers. Status badge: witnessed=green, planned=blue, open=orange. Evidence shown as collapsed text (expand on click). Loop links navigate to LoopTimeline.

InstrumentSpec.Description: Table: InstrumentSpec — the complete specification of the Simpson's Paradox classification instrument. One row per component: required input fields, derived coordinates, classification outputs, and adapter requirements. Every row is machine-verifiable against the 16 hydrated studies. This table is the distillation of loops 1–25 into a reusable tool.

schema.name: SpecId
schema.Description: Unique identifier for this instrument component.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ComponentType
schema.Description: input | derived | classification | adapter-requirement

schema.name: FieldName
schema.Description: The field or variable name in the DAG.

schema.name: DataType
schema.Description: integer | decimal | boolean | string | categorical

schema.name: SourceTable
schema.Description: The DAG table where this field is computed or stored.

schema.name: Formula
schema.Description: The ERB formula or derivation rule for this component.

schema.name: NaturalLanguage
schema.Description: Plain-English statement of what this component represents or requires.

schema.name: AdapterNote
schema.Description: What a researcher must supply or verify to satisfy this component when hydrating a new study.

data.NaturalLanguage: A unique identifier for the study being classified (e.g. 'kidney-1986').
data.AdapterNote: Assign a slug that is stable and unique. All rows for a study share this value.

data.NaturalLanguage: The label of the subgroup (stratum) for this cell — the variable being stratified on (e.g. 'large', 'high-risk', 'male').
data.AdapterNote: Must be consistent across the A and B cells within the same stratum. At least 2 strata required.

data.NaturalLanguage: Which treatment (exposure, group, class) this cell belongs to — 'A' or 'B'.
data.AdapterNote: Exactly two treatment labels per study. Encode the group you want to investigate as 'A'; comparator as 'B'.

data.NaturalLanguage: Count of successful outcomes (survived, passed, admitted, etc.) in this cell.
data.AdapterNote: Must be a non-negative integer ≤ Cases. Define 'success' consistently across all cells in the study.

data.NaturalLanguage: Total number of observations (trials, patients, applicants, etc.) in this cell.
data.AdapterNote: Must be a positive integer. Every (Study, Stratum, Treatment) combination must have exactly one CaseCell row.

data.NaturalLanguage: The success rate for this specific treatment within this specific stratum.
data.AdapterNote: Computed automatically. Range [0,1].

data.NaturalLanguage: The overall (pooled, unstratified) success rate for treatment A across all strata.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from SUMIFS across CaseCells. This is the rate a naive observer would report.

data.NaturalLanguage: The overall (pooled, unstratified) success rate for treatment B across all strata.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from SUMIFS across CaseCells.

data.NaturalLanguage: The signed difference in pooled rates (A minus B). Positive means A appears better pooled; negative means B appears better.
data.AdapterNote: The naive result — what the unadjusted analysis reports.

data.NaturalLanguage: The fraction of all treatment-A cases that fall in this stratum — treatment A's allocation weight.
data.AdapterNote: Sums to 1.0 across strata within a study.

data.NaturalLanguage: The signed per-stratum rate difference (A minus B). The true causal signal for this stratum, free of allocation effects.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from within-stratum aggregation of CaseCells.

data.NaturalLanguage: The stratum gap weighted by the average of A's and B's allocation fractions — the equal-weight contribution of this stratum.
data.AdapterNote: Summing across strata yields WeightedStratumGapSum.

data.NaturalLanguage: The equal-allocation benchmark: what SignedPooledGap would be if each treatment received the same case-mix. This is the allocation-free signal.
data.AdapterNote: Compare to SignedPooledGap to detect distortion.

data.NaturalLanguage: TRUE when the pooled winner (SignedPooledGap) points in the opposite direction from the equal-weight per-stratum winner (WeightedStratumGapSum). This is the sign of a reversal.
data.AdapterNote: The primary reversal detector.

data.NaturalLanguage: The absolute magnitude of the allocation distortion — how much the observed pooled gap differs from the equal-weight benchmark.
data.AdapterNote: Zero means no allocation confounding. > 0.01 is the classification threshold for types C and D.

data.NaturalLanguage: The four-type classification: A=full reversal (A wins all strata, B wins pooled); B=partial reversal (split strata, pooled contradicts weighted); C=distortion without reversal (same winner, but margin compressed or inflated by ≥1pp); D=trustworthy pooled (allocation-free or near-free).
data.AdapterNote: Output. Read-only for the researcher.

data.NaturalLanguage: The recommended analytic action given the distortion type: A→stratify immediately (pooled is misleading); B→investigate the confounder structure; C→report allocation bias alongside pooled result; D→pooled analysis is trustworthy.
data.AdapterNote: Output. This is the instrument's primary policy-facing deliverable.

data.NaturalLanguage: One Strata row per (study, stratum) combination, with a plain-English Description of what this subgroup represents and why it matters.
data.AdapterNote: Required for interpretability. The DAG computes rates without it, but the explanation of the paradox requires this context.

data.NaturalLanguage: One StratumVariables row per study naming the confounding variable, its CausalRole (confounder/mediator/contested), and a MechanismNote explaining how the allocation imbalance arises.
data.AdapterNote: CausalRole must be one of: confounder, mediator, contested, unknown. IsConfounder is derived (confounder AND both affect-flags true).

data.NaturalLanguage: Four StratumSummaries seed rows per study (one per stratum × treatment combination) — only StratumSummaryId, Study, StratumLabel, TreatmentLabel required; all computed fields are populated by the DAG.
data.AdapterNote: Minimum hydration contract: provide the four raw-key fields. The re-hydrate step fills in all derived values from the views.

AllocationSweep.Description: Table: AllocationSweep — parametric sweep of treatment-A allocation fraction within each study's most confounded stratum (see SweepStudyConfig). Ten fractions from 0.05 to 0.95 per study. SweepCorrectedGap is invariant; SweepPooledGap wanders.

schema.name: SweepId
schema.Description: Unique row identifier: <study>-f<pct> where pct is AllocFractionA*100.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: StudyId
schema.Description: The study being swept — all 40 studies in the corpus.

schema.name: AllocFractionA
schema.Description: Fraction of the sweep-stratum cases assigned to treatment A. Ranges from 0.05 to 0.95. The sweep stratum is the one whose allocation was most confounded in the original study.

schema.name: IsOriginal
schema.Description: TRUE for the row closest to the actual original study allocation.

schema.name: NSweepStratumTotal
schema.Description: Total cases in the sweep stratum (the stratum whose allocation is being varied). Fixed per study.

schema.name: NSweepA
schema.Description: Cases in the sweep stratum assigned to treatment A = ROUND(NSweepStratumTotal * AllocFractionA).

schema.name: NSweepB
schema.Description: Cases in the sweep stratum assigned to treatment B = NSweepStratumTotal - NSweepA.

schema.name: NFixedA
schema.Description: Total A-treatment cases in all NON-swept strata (held fixed across sweep rows). For kidney-1986 = 87 (small-stratum A). For berkeley-1973 = 1302 (depts B+C+D male). For compressed-synthetic = 80 (easy-stratum A). For balanced-synthetic = 100 (hard-stratum A).

schema.name: NFixedB
schema.Description: Total B-treatment cases in all NON-swept strata (held fixed across sweep rows). For kidney-1986 = 270 (small-stratum B). For berkeley-1973 = 993 (depts B+C+D female). For compressed-synthetic = 120 (easy-stratum B). For balanced-synthetic = 100 (hard-stratum B).

schema.name: SweepRateA
schema.Description: Per-cell success rate for treatment A in the sweep stratum. Fixed per study.

schema.name: SweepRateB
schema.Description: Per-cell success rate for treatment B in the sweep stratum. Fixed per study.

schema.name: FixedRateA
schema.Description: Weighted success rate for treatment A across all fixed (non-swept) strata. Constant per study.

schema.name: FixedRateB
schema.Description: Weighted success rate for treatment B across all fixed (non-swept) strata. Constant per study.

schema.name: SweepPooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for treatment A = (NSweepA * SweepRateA + NFixedA * FixedRateA) / (NSweepA + NFixedA). Changes as AllocFractionA varies.

schema.name: SweepPooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for treatment B = (NSweepB * SweepRateB + NFixedB * FixedRateB) / (NSweepB + NFixedB). Changes as AllocFractionA varies.

schema.name: SweepPooledGap
schema.Description: SweepPooledRateA minus SweepPooledRateB. Wanders as AllocFractionA varies because pooled rates mix stratum sizes unequally. Crosses zero at some allocation (the paradox reversal point).

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGap
schema.Description: Allocation-free signal: sum over strata of (n_s/N) * (rate_As - rate_Bs). Constant across the sweep by construction — the allocation variable does not appear. This is the conserved quantity under re-stratification.

schema.name: AllocationDistortionWitness
schema.Description: SweepPooledGap minus SweepCorrectedGap. The allocation distortion: how much the pooled signal has been inflated or deflated by differential stratum allocation. Zero only when allocation is balanced.

schema.name: SweepStudyId
schema.Description: FK to Studies.StudyId. One row per study in the AllocationSweep.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: DistortionTypeLabel
schema.Description: DistortionType for this study (A, B, C, or D) — provides context for what the sweep should show.

schema.name: CorrectedGapConstant
schema.Description: The allocation-free corrected gap for this study, computed analytically.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapMax
schema.Description: Maximum SweepCorrectedGap across all allocation fractions for this study.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapMin
schema.Description: Minimum SweepCorrectedGap across all allocation fractions for this study.

schema.name: SweepCorrectedGapRange
schema.Description: Range of SweepCorrectedGap across the sweep = Max - Min. Should be effectively zero (< 0.0001) for all studies if CorrectedGap is truly allocation-free.

schema.name: SweepPooledGapMax
schema.Description: Maximum SweepPooledGap across all allocation fractions for this study.

schema.name: SweepPooledGapMin
schema.Description: Minimum SweepPooledGap across all allocation fractions for this study.

schema.name: SweepPooledGapRange
schema.Description: Range of SweepPooledGap across the sweep = Max - Min. Should be large for Type A/B studies (pooled gap wanders) and smaller for Type D (balanced allocation = less wandering).

schema.name: PooledGapCrossesZero
schema.Description: TRUE when SweepPooledGapMin < 0 and SweepPooledGapMax > 0 — the pooled gap changes sign somewhere in the sweep, meaning the paradox can be created or destroyed by pure allocation.

schema.name: InvariantWitness
schema.Description: Summary witness statement: PASS if CorrectedGap is invariant (range < 0.0001), FAIL otherwise.
schema.formula: =IF({{SweepCorrectedGapRange}} < 0.0001, "PASS: CorrectedGap invariant across allocation sweep", "FAIL: CorrectedGap varies — formula error")

schema.name: SweepStratumLabel
schema.Description: Which stratum is being swept for this study.

ResearchTraditions.Description: Table: ResearchTraditions — the six scholarly lineages that have engaged with Simpson's Paradox. Each row is a named intellectual tradition, not a specific paper or result.

schema.name: TraditionId
schema.Description: Slug identifier, e.g. tradition-historical.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Short display name for the tradition.

schema.name: Era
schema.Description: Approximate active period, e.g. "1899–1972".

schema.name: CoreConcern
schema.Description: The central question this tradition asks about the paradox.

schema.name: PrimaryVenue
schema.Description: Where this tradition publishes (journal, conference, book series).

schema.name: KeyClaim
schema.Description: The tradition's signature thesis or resolution strategy.

schema.name: OpenQuestion
schema.Description: What this tradition leaves unresolved or contested.

schema.name: VerifiedByDeepResearch
schema.Description: True if the 2026-06-28 adversarial deep-research pass confirmed the tradition at high confidence.

schema.name: IllustratedByStudy
schema.Description: FK → Studies.StudyId. The study in this model that most vividly illustrates this tradition's characteristic concern or resolution strategy.

schema.name: SupportingConclusion
schema.Description: FK → Conclusions.ConclusionId. The conclusion in this model that most directly validates or advances this tradition's central claim about Simpson's Paradox.

schema.name: IllustratedByStudyTitle
schema.Description: Lookup: Studies.Title via IllustratedByStudy.

schema.name: IllustratedByStudySource
schema.Description: Lookup: Studies.Source via IllustratedByStudy — the citation for the study that best illustrates this tradition.

schema.name: IllustratedByStudyDistortionType
schema.Description: Lookup: TreatmentRankings.DistortionType for the illustrated study.

schema.name: SupportingConclusionTitle
schema.Description: Lookup: Conclusions.Title via SupportingConclusion.

schema.name: SupportingConclusionCategory
schema.Description: Lookup: Conclusions.Category via SupportingConclusion.

schema.name: ResearcherCount
schema.Description: Count of researchers in this tradition.

schema.name: StudyCount
schema.Description: Count of studies in this model whose TraditionId matches this tradition.

schema.name: LoopCount
schema.Description: Count of Leopold Loops that drew primarily on this tradition's conceptual vocabulary.

data.Name: Historical Statistical Origins
data.CoreConcern: Documenting that aggregate and partial associations can differ or reverse
data.PrimaryVenue: Biometrika; Journal of the Royal Statistical Society
data.KeyClaim: The reversal is a well-defined mathematical property of weighted averages, not a logical paradox — formalised by Blyth (1972)
data.OpenQuestion: Whether "disappearing association" (Pearson/Yule) and "sign reversal" (Cohen/Nagel) are the same phenomenon or two distinct effects

data.Name: Philosophy of Science / Probabilistic Causality
data.CoreConcern: Whether probabilistic associations alone can ground causal claims — the paradox is the stress test
data.PrimaryVenue: Philosophy of Science; Nous; Cambridge studies in probability/induction
data.KeyClaim: Cartwright (1979): causes must raise probabilities relative to ALL background contexts, not just the aggregate. Statistical accounts of causation fail without this constraint.
data.OpenQuestion: Whether the probabilistic causality framework is ultimately reducible to a causal graph account (Pearl) or remains genuinely distinct

data.Name: Causal Graphical / DAG Tradition
data.CoreConcern: Identifying the structural (graph) conditions under which stratification resolves or worsens the paradox
data.PrimaryVenue: JMLR; UAI; Artificial Intelligence; MIT Press books
data.KeyClaim: The paradox is a confounding problem solvable via the back-door criterion; a DAG over observed variables determines which table (pooled or stratified) is the correct one
data.OpenQuestion: Dong, Cai & Zhao (2024): confounding is not the universal source — inter-unit causation and accidental associations also produce the paradox, so DAG adjustment alone is insufficient

data.Name: Epidemiology / Observational Studies
data.CoreConcern: Noncollapsibility: when does controlling for a confounder change the measure of association, and when is that change meaningful?
data.PrimaryVenue: Epidemiology; American Journal of Epidemiology; Statistical Science
data.KeyClaim: Noncollapsibility is not identical to confounding — some measures (OR, HR) are noncollapsible even without confounding. Greenland (2021): distinguishing the two prevents spurious "paradox" diagnoses.
data.OpenQuestion: Which effect measures (RR, OR, RD) are collapsible under what conditions — an active debate in biostatistics

data.Name: Bayesian / Decision-Theoretic
data.CoreConcern: Whether Bayesian model-selection criteria (Bayes factors, posterior predictive) are themselves susceptible to Simpson-type reversals
data.PrimaryVenue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B; Bayesian Analysis
data.KeyClaim: Lindley (1997): Bayes factor proponents can simultaneously conclude tall men > tall women AND short men > short women AND women > men overall — a Simpson reversal in posterior odds. The paradox lives inside Bayesian inference, not just frequentist aggregation.
data.OpenQuestion: Whether prior specification can fully neutralise the reversal or whether the paradox is structurally unavoidable in hierarchical Bayesian models

data.Name: AI Fairness / Machine Learning
data.CoreConcern: Whether group-level fairness metrics are reliable when base rates differ across subgroups — Simpson's Paradox as a structural threat to audit validity
data.PrimaryVenue: FAccT; NeurIPS; Big Data journal; VLDB
data.KeyClaim: Chouldechova (2017) impossibility theorem: calibration and equal error rates are simultaneously unachievable when prevalence differs across groups. Differing base rates ARE the paradox mechanism in disguise.
data.OpenQuestion: Whether the FAccT community explicitly frames its impossibility results as Simpson's Paradox instances or treats base-rate divergence as a distinct phenomenon

Researchers.Description: Table: Researchers — individuals who have made named, verified contributions to understanding Simpson's Paradox. Linked to ResearchTraditions by TraditionId.

schema.name: ResearcherId

schema.name: TraditionId

schema.name: TraditionEra

schema.name: CanonicalStudyTitle

schema.name: CanonicalStudyPolicyImplication

data.Name: Karl Pearson
data.KeyContribution: First (1899) observed that marginal and partial associations between continuous variables can differ, producing spurious correlations — the pre-Simpson precursor.
data.SignatureWork: Pearson et al. (1899) — early work on genetic inheritance and spurious correlation
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Predates both; Pearl's DAG tradition explicitly cites Pearson as the origin point of the confusion his framework resolves

data.Name: G. Udny Yule
data.KeyContribution: Extended Pearson's observation (1903) to categorical data; the phenomenon is sometimes called the Yule–Simpson effect.
data.SignatureWork: Yule (1903) — Notes on the theory of association of attributes in statistics
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Provides the categorical-data framing that Hannan's aggregation bias literature builds on; Pearl's back-door criterion addresses exactly what Yule's correlations could not distinguish

data.Name: Cohen & Nagel
data.KeyContribution: First documented sign reversal specifically (association flipping direction, not merely disappearing), predating Simpson's 1951 paper.
data.SignatureWork: Cohen & Nagel (1934) — An Introduction to Logic and Scientific Method
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Provided the logical/philosophical framing that the philosophy-of-science tradition (Cartwright, Eells) built on

data.Name: Edward H. Simpson
data.KeyContribution: Gave the paradox its name by formalising sign reversal in contingency tables (1951 JRSS-B paper), though unaware of Cohen & Nagel's earlier sign-reversal documentation.
data.SignatureWork: Simpson (1951) — The Interpretation of Interaction in Contingency Tables, JRSS-B
data.RelationToHannanPearl: The naming origin; Pearl's r414.pdf distinguishes Simpson's formal contribution from the deeper causal question Simpson himself did not answer

data.Name: C. R. Blyth
data.KeyContribution: Coined the term "Simpson's Paradox" (1972), provided the first formal definition, and framed the reversal as a genuine logical paradox rather than a statistical curiosity.
data.SignatureWork: Blyth (1972) — On Simpson's Paradox and the Sure-Thing Principle, JASA
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Blyth's formal definition is one of three non-equivalent definitions in the literature (Blyth 1972, Mittal 1991, Bandyopadhyay et al. 2011); Pearl's framework dissolves the definitional ambiguity via causal structure

data.Name: Hans Reichenbach
data.KeyContribution: Developed the Common Cause Principle (1956): every statistical correlation has a causal explanation, either direct causation or a common cause. This principle frames the paradox as a symptom of an uncontrolled common cause.
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Pearl's backdoor criterion is a formalisation of Reichenbach's common-cause screening-off idea in DAG language

data.Name: Patrick Suppes
data.KeyContribution: Developed probabilistic causality theory (1970): causes raise the probability of their effects. The paradox shows this is necessary but insufficient — background context must be held fixed.
data.SignatureWork: Suppes (1970) — A Probabilistic Theory of Causality
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Suppes's framework is the direct predecessor Cartwright critiques and extends; Pearl's do-calculus supersedes probabilistic causality by making interventions explicit

data.Name: Nancy Cartwright
data.KeyContribution: Proposed the influential resolution (1979): a cause must raise the probability of its effect in every homogeneous background context, not just in the aggregate. Argued purely statistical accounts of causation fail.
data.SignatureWork: Cartwright (1979) — Causal Laws and Effective Strategies, Nous
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Cartwright's "background contexts" anticipate stratification in Pearl's sense; Pearl acknowledges Cartwright but argues DAGs make the notion precise and computable

data.Name: Ellery Eells
data.KeyContribution: Defended Cartwright's resolution against Richard Otte's (1985) critique; published Probabilistic Causality (1991), the most systematic treatment of the probabilistic causality tradition.
data.SignatureWork: Eells (1991) — Probabilistic Causality, Cambridge University Press
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Eells's defence of Cartwright against Otte closes off one branch of the philosophy debate; Pearl's DAG approach is seen by many as the operationalisation that finally settled it

data.Name: Spirtes, Glymour & Scheines
data.KeyContribution: The CMU group (SGS) developed constraint-based causal discovery (PC/FCI algorithms) as a parallel DAG tradition to Pearl's. Their 2000 book explicitly names clarifying Simpson's Paradox as a goal.
data.SignatureWork: Spirtes, Glymour & Scheines (2000) — Causation, Prediction, and Search, MIT Press
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Methodologically distinct from Pearl: SGS uses constraint-based structure discovery from conditional independence tests; Pearl uses do-calculus and interventional logic. Both treat the paradox as a confounding problem but differ in how to identify confounders from data.

data.Name: Dong, Cai & Zhao
data.KeyContribution: Challenged Pearl's confounding-centric account (2024): identified three classes of Simpson's Paradox that occur without confounding — inter-unit causation, accidental associations, and inappropriate aggregate variables.
data.SignatureWork: Dong, Cai & Zhao (2024) — European Journal for Philosophy of Science
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Direct critique of Pearl: argues the DAG back-door criterion is insufficient as a universal resolution because not all reversals are confounding artifacts

data.Name: Sander Greenland
data.KeyContribution: Linked Simpson's Paradox to noncollapsibility (2021): the failure of a group-level measure to equal a simple average of subgroup measures. Distinguishes noncollapsibility from confounding — the two are commonly conflated.
data.SignatureWork: Greenland (2021) — Noncollapsibility, confounding, and sparse-data bias, Journal of Clinical Epidemiology
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Collaborated with Pearl (Greenland, Robins & Pearl 1999, Statistical Science) on confounding and collapsibility. Greenland operationalises the paradox for epidemiological practice where Pearl's do-calculus is abstract.

data.Name: James M. Robins
data.KeyContribution: Developed marginal structural models and g-computation — causal inference methods for observational epidemiology that account for time-varying confounding, of which Simpson's Paradox is the canonical simple case.
data.SignatureWork: Greenland, Robins & Pearl (1999) — Confounding and Collapsibility in Causal Inference, Statistical Science
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Robins's g-methods and Pearl's do-calculus were developed independently and are now understood to be equivalent under certain conditions; both address the same confounding problem the paradox exposes

data.Name: Dennis Lindley
data.KeyContribution: Showed (1997) that Bayes factor proponents can simultaneously conclude tall men > tall women, short men > short women, yet women > men overall — a Simpson reversal inside Bayesian hypothesis testing. Lindley himself noted the connection.
data.SignatureWork: Lindley (1997) — counter-example to Aitkin's posterior Bayes factor, JRSS-B
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Orthogonal to both Hannan and Pearl: Lindley shows the paradox is not merely a frequentist aggregation problem but appears inside the Bayesian framework's own model-selection machinery

data.Name: Murray Aitkin
data.Affiliation: University of Newcastle / University of Western Australia
data.KeyContribution: Applied Simpson's Paradox to posterior Bayes factors; his work prompted Lindley's counter-example and is the proximate occasion for the Bayesian tradition's engagement with the paradox.
data.SignatureWork: Aitkin (1991/1997) — posterior Bayes factor papers, JRSS-B
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Part of an internal Bayesian dispute; the paradox appears as a consistency problem in model selection, not as an aggregation or confounding problem in Pearl's sense

data.Name: Alexandra Chouldechova
data.KeyContribution: Proved the fairness impossibility theorem (2017): when recidivism prevalence differs across groups, satisfying calibration necessarily produces disparate error rates — base-rate divergence (the paradox mechanism) makes irreconcilable fairness criteria structurally unavoidable.
data.SignatureWork: Chouldechova (2017) — Fair Prediction with Disparate Impact, Big Data journal (arXiv:1610.07524)
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Translates the paradox into ML fairness: the "confounder" is group membership; the "strata" are demographic subgroups; the reversal is which group appears disadvantaged depending on the metric chosen. Not framed in Pearl's DAG language but structurally equivalent.

data.Name: Abolfazl Asudeh
data.KeyContribution: Teaches and researches Simpson's Paradox as a core topic in responsible data science and AI fairness; frames it as a foundational structural threat to fairness audits in heterogeneous datasets.
data.SignatureWork: CS516 course materials and related research at UIC (2025)
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Bridges the Hannan aggregation-bias framing and the AI fairness audit literature; treats the paradox as a data-engineering problem, not purely a causal inference problem

data.Name: Sharma, Kaushik, Peious & Draheim
data.Affiliation: Tallinn University of Technology — Information Systems Group
data.KeyContribution: Extended Simpson's Paradox detection from categorical to continuous variables for ML fairness audits (2022); frames automated paradox detection as a step toward next-generation fairness platforms.
data.SignatureWork: Sharma et al. (2022) — Detecting Simpson's Paradox: A Step Towards Fairness in Machine Learning, Springer
data.RelationToHannanPearl: Extends Hannan-style aggregation detection into continuous feature spaces; does not use Pearl's causal framework but treats the paradox as a data-pattern detection problem

SyntheticPhase.Description: Table: SyntheticPhase — a parametric grid of canonical 2×2 synthetic studies at controlled (ParamStratumFraction, ParamStratumGap1, ParamStratumGap2, ParamAllocationBias) coordinates. Stratum 1 is the hard stratum (base rate 0.45); stratum 2 is easy (base rate 0.75). Each row constructs cell counts from the parameters and derives PhaseDistortionType using the same geometric classification as TreatmentRankings. This maps the A/B/C+/C-/D phase boundaries algebraically — independent of empirical corpus data.

schema.name: PhaseId
schema.Description: Unique grid-point identifier encoding (w, g1, g2, b) coordinates.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ParamStratumFraction
schema.Description: w — fraction of total cases in the hard (stratum-1) subgroup. Range 0.30–0.75 in this grid.

schema.name: ParamStratumGap1
schema.Description: g1 — signed treatment advantage (rate_A − rate_B) in the hard stratum. Positive means A wins stratum 1.

schema.name: ParamStratumGap2
schema.Description: g2 — signed treatment advantage in the easy stratum. Negative g2 enables type-B (partial reversal) cells.

schema.name: ParamAllocationBias
schema.Description: b — allocation bias in stratum 1: extra fraction of stratum-1 cases assigned to treatment A above 50/50. Range 0.00–0.95.

schema.name: PhaseS1Total
schema.Description: Total cases in stratum 1 = ROUND(200 × ParamStratumFraction).

schema.name: PhaseNA1
schema.Description: Treatment-A cases in stratum 1.

schema.name: PhaseNB1
schema.Description: Treatment-B cases in stratum 1.

schema.name: PhaseNA2
schema.Description: Treatment-A cases in stratum 2.

schema.name: PhaseNB2
schema.Description: Treatment-B cases in stratum 2.

schema.name: PhaseRateA1
schema.Description: Success rate for A in hard stratum.

schema.name: PhaseRateB1
schema.Description: Success rate for B in hard stratum.

schema.name: PhaseRateA2
schema.Description: Success rate for A in easy stratum.

schema.name: PhaseRateB2
schema.Description: Success rate for B in easy stratum.

schema.name: PhasePooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for treatment A.

schema.name: PhasePooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for treatment B.

schema.name: PhaseSignedPooledGap
schema.Description: Pooled rate gap (A minus B).

schema.name: PhaseCorrectedGap
schema.Description: Allocation-free equal-weight gap = w×g1 + (1−w)×g2.

schema.name: PhaseStrataWonByLoser
schema.Description: Count of strata whose per-stratum winner disagrees with the pooled winner.

schema.name: PhaseReversalIntensity
schema.Description: PhaseStrataWonByLoser / 2 for this 2-stratum template.

schema.name: PhaseIsSignFlip
schema.Description: TRUE when equal-weight and pooled gaps have opposite signs.

schema.name: PhaseAllocationDistortion
schema.Description: |PhaseCorrectedGap − PhaseSignedPooledGap|.

schema.name: PhaseDistortionType
schema.Description: Five-type geometric classification at this parameter point.
schema.formula: =IF(AND({{PhaseIsSignFlip}}, {{PhaseReversalIntensity}} = 1), "A", IF(AND({{PhaseIsSignFlip}}, {{PhaseReversalIntensity}} < 1), "B", IF(AND(NOT({{PhaseIsSignFlip}}), {{PhaseAllocationDistortion}} > 0.01, ABS({{PhaseSignedPooledGap}}) > ABS({{PhaseCorrectedGap}}) + 0.001), "C+", IF(AND(NOT({{PhaseIsSignFlip}}), {{PhaseAllocationDistortion}} > 0.01, ABS({{PhaseSignedPooledGap}}) < ABS({{PhaseCorrectedGap}}) - 0.001), "C-", "D"))))

PhaseDiagramSummary.Description: Table: PhaseDiagramSummary — one row summarising the SyntheticPhase grid. Witnesses that all five DistortionType cells (A, B, C+, C-, D) are nonempty in parameter space.

schema.name: PhaseDiagramId

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: PhasePointCount

schema.name: PhaseTypeACount
schema.Description: Grid points classified as type A. Populated from vw_synthetic_phase by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: PhaseTypeBCount
schema.Description: Grid points classified as type B. Populated from vw_synthetic_phase by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: PhaseTypeCPlusCount
schema.Description: Grid points classified as type C+. Populated from vw_synthetic_phase by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: PhaseTypeCMinusCount
schema.Description: Grid points classified as type C-. Populated from vw_synthetic_phase by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: PhaseTypeDCount
schema.Description: Grid points classified as type D. Populated from vw_synthetic_phase by 05b-customize-data.sql.

schema.name: AllFiveTypesPresent
schema.Description: TRUE when every DistortionType cell has at least one grid point.

schema.name: PhaseTaxonomyWitness
schema.Description: Human-readable witness string of type counts across the grid.

schema.name: PhaseWitnessNote
schema.Description: PASS/FAIL summary for taxonomy completeness.
schema.formula: =IF({{AllFiveTypesPresent}}, "PASS: all five distortion types populated in parameter space", "FAIL: missing distortion type in grid")

schema.Description: How to verify compliance before classification.

schema.name: SortOrder

data.RequirementStatement: Every cell row must name its parent study with a stable unique slug.
data.MechanicalCheck: Non-empty string; identical across all cells in the study; matches Studies.StudyId.

data.RequirementStatement: Every cell must name its stratum — the subgroup being stratified on.
data.MechanicalCheck: Non-empty string; consistent label for both A and B cells within the same stratum.

data.RequirementStatement: Every cell must be labeled A or B — exactly two treatments per study.
data.MechanicalCheck: Value in {A, B}; both labels appear.

data.RequirementStatement: Total observations in the cell — the denominator for the success rate.
Count of successful outcomes in the cell — the numerator.
Exactly two treatment arms (A and B) per study.
data.MechanicalCheck: Exactly two distinct TreatmentLabel values in CaseCells.

data.RequirementStatement: Every stratum must have exactly one A-cell and one B-cell.
Published or citable 2xK contingency table with aggregate counts.
data.MechanicalCheck: Source citation present; counts are integers.

data.RequirementStatement: Binary outcome encodable as Successes out of Cases.
Exclude continuous outcomes without stated dichotomisation.
Exclude post-hoc stratum splitting to force a reversal.
data.MechanicalCheck: Stratum variable named in original publication.

data.RequirementStatement: One Strata row per (study, stratum) with Description.

data.MechanicalCheck: Exactly two Treatments rows per study.

data.RequirementStatement: One StratumVariables row with CausalRole and MechanismNote.

schema.Description: Valid CaseCells.

schema.Description: Studies with IngestionCellParity.

schema.name: AllStudiesStructural

schema.Description: Non-synthetic studies.

schema.name: RealFullyCompliantCount

schema.Description: All real studies fully compliant.
